title: Spearfish mayor addresses citizen questions during city council meetings
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Spearfish Mayor John Senden, pictured, is asking for the public to submit their questions on city matters via email or over the phone, prior to city council meetings. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
Spearfish mayor addresses citizen questions during city council meetings
Spearfish Mayor John Senden, pictured, is asking for the public to submit their questions on city matters via email or over the phone, prior to city council meetings. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

Q&A with Spearfish Mayor John Senden on new questions policy
Q: Citizens will now have the opportunity to ask questions during regular city council meetings, if they sign up at the beginning of the meeting and/or submit questions via email ahead of time. How did you decide on this method?    
 A: The Monday night council meetings are business meetings for approving expenditures, contracts, ordinances, and other various items brought to council for a vote and final decision. Some citizens who prepare themselves for the meeting and want to ask a question will now have the opportunity to have it be done in public, or possibly it can be done by an elected official or department chair prior to the council meeting.
 Q: Will questions asked via email be answered at the city council meeting?  
 A: We will do some of both, I believe. Some questions are more asked for clarification, others directed on financial impact will likely be part of the discussion in council, so the public is made aware. 
 Q: Will the same policy apply to study sessions? 
 A: Study sessions should allow for more back and forth discussions since we are not voting on any agenda item in these meetings. By way of example, our recent meeting to review all of the TIF’s the city has ever done was open to discussions after each TIF was presented by our Finance Officer. We had citizens come up for questions on several of the items and we asked some of those in attendance to come up and speak. This can be part of the dialogue too as we review the upcoming council agenda. 
 Q: Did you consider opening the floor for questions after items are presented, and timing the question and answer process? Why or why not?  
 A: To a degree this is already in place. When we have had items of interest, such as snow removal, there was open public comment allowed right after the staff reports were made. I think in the last year I have done this 4 or 5 times, but some citizens have expressed an interest in asking question regarding “city spending of tax dollars” and wanting timely input during open meetings. That is the big reason I am proposing this change, while also trying to keep the business meeting a business meeting.
 Q: Do you think this new system will help, often busy, constituents to be more engaged with the council and its decision making process? Why or why not?
 A: I am hopeful it will help the citizens who are concerned about accountability to feel they can get their questions addressed in public, while there is also some control of the meeting so that whoever is chairing the meeting can keep it flowing to get important decisions made. And citizens can always engage with me and the council at any time during the week via email or a phone call. 
 Q: Citizens ask questions in open session to give their neighbors a chance to hear the same answers. Does this method help serve that purpose?  
 A: Yes. We will want to make sure that questions of financial impact definitely get out into the public, even if the person asking the question does not want to stand up at the podium. We want to honor the public’s right to have financial accountability. But for those questions that are more for clarification, it is possible that the response may be made just to that individual. 
 Q: If you or the council receive, for example, 10 different questions from a constituent, are you going to answer them all at the meeting?   
 A: It would depend on the questions, of course. If this were to happen, I think a call prior to the meeting to find out what is the most important item or two would be appropriate.
 Q: If a question is answered privately in an email or phone call, is there a risk that the council will not be made aware of a citizen’s concern or confusion on an important topic? 
 A: If a citizen privately discusses a question or concern that deals with a topic of general concern, we would expect the council member responding to that request to note that issue and response during discussion on the topic. At this point we will have to see how the process plays out, but I think that getting feedback on how to best share information between all elected officials will be worked out as we use this process.
 Q: Are you prepared to answer questions in time for a meeting Monday if they’re sent on Friday after the agenda packet is posted? Has council expressed that they are willing to provide constituents with answers on that timeline? 
 A: I expect we will be prepared for the Friday questions for sure, but if questions come in late we may need to establish some form of deadline for submissions. Council days can be very busy for elected officials and directors, so some advance time is needed to be prepared. Again, this is somewhat of a work in progress. I don’t know of any other local legislative board taking these steps. With the call from some for the ability to get questions answered this is our attempt to take steps in meeting those requests. The board meeting chair will run the meeting and read off questions posed or invite guests forward who have submitted questions. 
 Q: This legislative session, Rep. Mary Fitzgerald, R- St. Onge, brought a bill (HB1091) that would have required local governments to post complete agendas 24 hours prior to a meeting (without major amendments) and to correct any errors or update any out-of-date materials in agenda packets within 24 hours. That bill died in committee. Without constraints ensuring that citizens have access to a complete agenda, are you concerned that constituents might miss an opportunity to ask a question about an issue that matters to them?    
 A: The final agenda for the regular Monday evening council meetings is posted on Friday afternoon, approximately 72 hours before the Monday meetings. Additionally, tentative agenda is posted prior to the council’s study session, which is held on Wednesday, five days prior to the regular Monday meeting. This gives interested citizens ample time to understand what is on the agenda, and to contact the city if they have questions. I want people to have the right to know what is being considered on an upcoming business meeting and give input they feel matters to them. This is a two-way street, the city needs to provide ample opportunity with a system that informs, and the citizens must make their best effort to get informed. Once they get the information, calls to any of the elected official, presenting their questions prior to the meeting or attending the meeting is their right and opportunity to have their voices heard.
 Q: You noted on March 2 that you would like to recommit to transparency. What does transparency mean to you?  
 A: As elected officials our decisions are made in public meetings. Elected officials need to have a listening ear and respond to appropriate inquiries from the public. We are responsible to the citizens and we want all people to be informed publicly about the reasons why decisions are made. The public wants well thought out decisions with the long-range view in mind.
 Q: What kinds of information do you feel you are not obligated to offer in the name of transparency? 
 A: South Dakota a law spells out the types of discussions that can or should be held in executive or closed meetings. It also identifies several documents and information that are protected from public disclosure.  We work to make sure that information which should be protected is protected. Beyond that, we want to ensure the public has visibility to the information they need and want.

SPEARFISH — Spearfish citizens wishing to ask questions of the mayor, city leadership, or the city council in open, regular city council meetings have a new procedure they’ll need to follow to receive answers in public.

For weeks, community members like Doug Markworth have asked the mayor for the opportunity to ask questions of city leadership about items on the agenda during city council meetings.

“Hopefully these (questions) are not rhetorical. I would love to have a conversation,” Markworth said. “It seems like the council would benefit from input from citizens with appropriately timed questions.”

Traditionally, the public comment period offers citizens the chance to address the public and the council on the record. The council cannot take action on any comments that are not directly related to agenda items. Generally, the council and mayor do not respond to questions citizens ask during the public comment period.

Spearfish Mayor John Senden said the council will allow residents to ask direct questions — if they plan ahead.

“I want us to be able to hear from the public, but I also want to make sure that we have a basis of consistency. I think this is going to provide some clarity moving forward,” Senden said.

Senden encouraged people to reach out via phone or email, directly to their city leaders if they have questions on city business.

“If a person simply wants an answer to a question that can be done with a phone call or email,” Senden said. “If a citizen would rather ask the question themselves at the city council, they can send an email to our executive assistant, Lita Eddy, or the mayor, or sign in just prior to the start of the meeting for a specific item on the agenda, and they will be allowed to ask their question personally and publicly when that item comes up on the agenda.”

The process, then, would be a bit like speaking during public hearings. Citizens will need to sign in with questions before a meeting is gavelled in. 

“Having some form of order to the proposed questions during council will be thoughtful and allow the chair of the meeting to invite the person forward at an appropriate time for their question,” Senden said.

City council meetings generally take place every other Monday at 5:30 p.m. at city hall. The agenda is posted by 5:30 p.m. on the Friday prior.

A draft version of the Spearfish City Council regular session agenda is generally attached to the study session agenda.  Study sessions take place the Wednesday before city council meetings at 4:15 p.m.

Study session agendas must legally be posted at least 24 hours before the scheduled meeting — by 4:15 p.m. on the Tuesday before the session. Senden said study session agendas are usually posted sooner than that — by noon on Tuesdays.

He argued that, because the public can access a draft agenda for up to 6 days before a city council meeting, there is time for residents to consider discussion items and prepare questions for their elected officials in advance.

“Just as mayor and council have to prepare themselves for the meeting, citizens who have prepared themselves for the meeting will be able to, in advance of the meeting, prepare their questions, and notify the chairperson of their intent to ask that question,” Senden said. “There is transparency in the amount of time the agenda is published, plus the fact that communication with the public prior to a meeting is welcome.”

Senden said the process for asking questions may change moving forward, but will be in place for now.  Senden said the study sessions will remain more “free form” than regular sessions, framing the council meetings as “business meetings” that necessitate more structure.

“Let’s give this change a chance,” Senden said.

title: RC man sentenced to prison on  drug charges
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Gavel01
Courtesy photo 

RAPID CITY — A Rapid City man was sentenced to nearly 16 years in federal prison for his role in distributing methamphetamine and fentanyl.

Zachariah Highelk, 35, was sentenced Monday to 15 years and eight months in federal prison, followed by five years of supervised release, and ordered to pay a $100 special assessment to the Federal Crime Victims Fund.

Highelk was indicted for conspiracy to distribute a controlled substance by a federal grand jury in February 2025. He pleaded guilty on Dec. 3, 2025.

Between 2019 and 2024, Highelk took trips to Colorado and Washington to meet with his sources of supply to purchase bulk amounts of methamphetamine and fentanyl. He would then return to Rapid City and begin distributing the drugs to others in the community.

Highelk was responsible for multiple pounds of methamphetamine and thousands of fentanyl pills being distributed throughout the Rapid City community, spreading on to Indian reservations in the state.

“This was an incredible takedown by South Dakota and federal law enforcement that we believe put a substantial dent in the drug trade,” said U.S. Attorney Ron Parsons. “For any methamphetamine or fentanyl ingested in western South Dakota over the past several years, there is a decent chance it passed through this drug dealer’s hands at some point. He will spend the next fifteen years reflecting on that from a federal prison cell. On to the next.”

This case is part of Operation Take Back America, a nationwide initiative that marshals the full resources of the Department of Justice to repel the invasion of illegal immigration, achieve the total elimination of cartels and transnational criminal organizations (TCOs), and protect our communities from the perpetrators of violent crime. Operation Take Back America streamlines efforts and resources from the Department’s Organized Crime Drug Enforcement Task Forces (OCDETFs) and Project Safe Neighborhood (PSN).

This case was investigated by the Unified Narcotics Enforcement Team (UNET), the Drug Enforcement Administration, and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. UNET is comprised of law enforcement from the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, the Rapid City Police Department, South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation, the South Dakota Highway Patrol, and the South Dakota National Guard. Assistant U.S. Attorney Paige Petersen prosecuted the case.

Highelk was immediately remanded to the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service.

title: New role as CEO a  ‘return home’ for Northern Hills Training Center’s Laura Wendland
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Laura Wendland is a familiar face at Northern Hills Training Center, where she worked for 20 years. This month she returned to lead the organization as its new CEO. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
New role as CEO a  ‘return home’ for Northern Hills Training Center’s Laura Wendland
Laura Wendland is a familiar face at Northern Hills Training Center, where she worked for 20 years. This month she returned to lead the organization as its new CEO. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — Northern Hills Training Center (NHTC) serves individuals with developmental disabilities with residential care, day services, and employment opportunities. The new CEO, Laura Wendland, said the organization is a cornerstone of the community because of those services, and its capacity to “create belonging.”

Wendland should know. She worked for NHTC for 20 years before departing in 2016; she returned on March 9 as CEO.

“We support adults with disabilities to live meaningful, connected lives, and we employ a dedicated workforce that strengthens the social and economic fabric of the region. When people with disabilities are included, supported, and empowered, the entire community becomes stronger, more compassionate, and more vibrant,” Wendland said. “NHTC is not just a service provider. We are neighbors, partners, and contributors to the shared life of Spearfish and the Northern Hills. Our community is better because of the people we support and the staff who walk alongside them.

When Wendland started her career at NHTC in 1996, she was a direct support professional (DSP).

“A direct support professional is the heart of disability services. DSPs support people with intellectual and developmental disabilities in the most essential parts of daily life — building skills, fostering independence, nurturing relationships, and helping people live full, selfdirected lives,” Wendland said. “It remains the role that taught me everything I know about leadership: listening deeply, honoring autonomy, and showing up with consistency and compassion.”

Wendland previously led NHTC’s day program. Then she moved to case management — a department she managed for 13 years.

“Today, as CEO, my work looks different — but the purpose is the same. Instead of supporting individuals directly, I’m responsible for building the systems, culture, and stability that allow DSPs and the people we support to thrive. My job now is to remove barriers, strengthen our workforce, and ensure that the mission is lived out in every home, every program, every relationship,” Wendland said.

After a stint at Black Hills Works, Wendland described her return to NHTC as a homecoming.

“My relationship with NHTC and this field goes back more than three decades. In many ways, it feels like coming home to the work and the people who shaped my entire career,” Wendland said.

The biggest challenges faced by NHTC are the same challenges disability service providers are staring down across the country.

Workforce stability, specifically DSP burnout, is a problem. So are funding structures that “haven’t kept pace with the true cost of quality support.”

“Balancing financial stability with personcentered excellence is a constant tension — but it’s also where strong leadership matters most,” Wednland said. “My focus is on building predictable operations, strengthening our workforce, and ensuring that every decision protects the people we support and the staff who show up for them every day.”

She said there is room for modernization in the field, but deep, personal relationships are the core of successful disability services, Wendland said.

“I’m most energized by the opportunity to shape the next era of NHTC — one rooted in belonging, stability, and innovation,” Wendland said. “I look forward to elevating the voices of people supported and DSPs; strengthening community partnerships so inclusion becomes the norm, not the exception; modernizing our systems so staff feel supported and people receive the highest quality of care; and building a culture where everyone — staff and people supported — feels valued, seen, and connected.This role allows me to bring together everything I love: people, purpose, and the chance to build something that lasts.”

title: DOE gives SURF $7M more  to refurbish Yates Hoist
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: The moon rises over the Yates Shaft at the Sanford Underground Research Facility in Lead. This year, the SDSTA will focus on a major refurbishment project for the 90-year old Yates Hoist. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick
_full_text:
moonrise over Yates.jpg
The moon rises over the Yates Shaft at the Sanford Underground Research Facility in Lead. This year, the SDSTA will focus on a major refurbishment project for the 90-year old Yates Hoist. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick 

 sunset over Yates.jpg
The sun sets over the Yates Shaft in Lead. This year, the SDSTA will focus on a major refurbishment project for the 90-year old Yates Hoist. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick  

LEAD — A $7 million boost in federal funds will allow the Sanford Underground Research Facility to start upgrading the Yates Hoist. 

On Thursday, the S.D. Science and Technology Authority (SDSTA) approved an increase in its 2026 budget, to account for the extra funding it will receive from the U.S. Department of Energy. Normally, the SDSTA budgets $35 million in federal operating funds, but in 2026 the DOE approved $44 million. The majority of the extra funding was specifically allocated to be used on upgrading the Yates Hoist, which is used to access the Sanford Underground Research Facility through the Yates Shaft. 

“We’re operating on basically 90-year old technology,” Headley told the Pioneer. “I’m not sure I know of many operations that are doing a 24-7, gotta be up and running, all the time thing, on 90-year old electric gear. It has been incredibly reliable and served this place well for decades. But the challenge is that there are a number of components that we don’t have spares for.” 

While work on the Yates Hoist is expected to begin this year, Headley said it will be an ongoing project that lab officials hope to have completed before the end of the decade. Headley said the SDSTA is working with Al Stratman and Stantec Engineering to put together a conceptual design for the project, to prepare to advertise for a design-build project. The project will include two phases. This year the SDSTA will start replacing the electrical gear, motors and controls in the hoist system, for the first phase. In subsequent years, Headley said they will move into replacing clutches, brakes and other mechanical systems for the hoist. 

The upgrades are the next step toward rehabilitating the Yates Complex. In 2025, SURF officials celebrated the re-opening of the Yates Shaft after nearly 4 years of limited availability, as crews worked to replace timber and rock bolts with what they referred to as top-down maintenance. Chief Operating Officer Wendy Straub was at the helm of that project for which crews replaced 50% of the timber in the shaft, and 75% of the rock bolting. The project completion was a major milestone for the lab that has been using the Ross Shaft as its primary access into the underground lab since 2021. 

Re-opening the Yates Shaft means that crews will use it to move more than 6 million pounds of steel beams and related materials underground to build the cryostats for the deep underground neutrino experiment. The increased usage makes upgrades to the Yates Hoist even more important this year, to ensure its regular reliability. 

In the meantime, Headley reported that additional funding that has been allocated for the Long Baseline Neutrino Facility/Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment is taking away from dark matter research. The DOE, Headley said, has “taken a pause” for additional funding for the next generation dark matter experiment that is planned to succeed the LUX-ZEPLIN (LZ), which could conclude operations in 2028. 

“With that being said, there is significant interest by the DOE in funding continued operations for the LZ experiment, the existing dark matter experiment that we have,” Headley said.

title: Deadwood passes special assessments ordinance, denies motion to continue
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — At the March 16 Deadwood City Commission meeting, commissioners passed the second reading of an ordinance allowing the city to perform special assessments and denied a motion made to continue the ordinance to a later meeting.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

DEADWOOD — At the March 16 Deadwood City Commission meeting, commissioners passed the second reading of an ordinance allowing the city to perform special assessments and denied a motion made to continue the ordinance to a later meeting.

Quentin Riggins, city attorney, said that the lengthy ordinance was written in compliance with state statutes, allowing the city to make special assessments and appeals to fund future developments, as another “tool in the toolbox.”

“It’s largely modeled after the one the city of Brandon uses,” said Riggins. “It does not mean we have to use this procedure. To my knowledge, there aren’t any pending requests to issue a special assessment. It’s just a method we can use for future developments.”

Commissioner Mark Speirs asked Riggins how the ordinance will help the city’s planning and zoning department. 

“There is procedure in it for replacement of utility — water, sewer, sidewalk. This could all be used to make upgrades to existing developments if that’s something we so choose to do in future,” said Riggins. 

Speirs affirmed that the special assessment is not mandatory for future developments. 

Commissioner Charles Eagleson moved to continue the ordinance until the April 6 meeting, stating that he’s received questions from constituents that he wanted to research with department heads before enacting the ordinance.

Commissioner Sharon Martinisko asked if he brought those questions to the commission.

“Because, respectfully, you’re always talking about transparency, so I’m thinking now is a good time to bring those questions forward,” Martinisko said. “I’m sure (Riggins) will be able to answer them.”

Eagleson did not bring the questions to the commission at that time, reiterating that he would prefer to work with department heads over the next 2 weeks to get answers from department heads in work sessions. 

The motion to continue the ordinance was seconded by Speirs but died with a 2-3 vote. Martinisko, Commissioner Mike Johnson, and Mayor Charlie Struble-Mook voted to deny the motion. 

“There has been a lot of work that has been put into this,” said Martinisko. “It is not a requirement that the city use it, as Kevin (Kuchenbecker, director of planning and zoning and historic preservation) and (Riggins) have stated in the past, it is another tool in the toolbox to protect the city and our residents so they don’t have to bear the new additional costs for a development. It is well written, and there is nowhere in it that says it has to be used. Yes, it is very lengthy, but it is very well written.”

Speirs said that a “particular developer” in the community was unaware of the ordinance and had questions that Speirs and Eagleson felt should be answered before the ordinance was passed. 

Martinisko moved to pass the second reading of the ordinance, which Johnson seconded. The motion passed 3-2, with Eagleson and Speirs voting against it.

title: State opens applications for 40 more slots in teacher apprenticeship program
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — Another 40 paraprofessionals could be accepted into South Dakota’s teacher apprenticeship program, which is meant to reduce the teaching shortage across the state.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — Another 40 paraprofessionals could be accepted into South Dakota’s teacher apprenticeship program, which is meant to reduce the teaching shortage across the state. 

The program, started in 2023, allows full-time paraprofessionals — sometimes called teacher’s aides — to pursue a teaching degree online through Dakota State University in elementary or special education, or through Northern State University in secondary education, at a steep discount while retaining their position.

Gov. Larry Rhoden said in a news release that the program is a “strong partnership” between the state and its universities to address a statewide issue.

“When paraprofessionals can earn a degree right in their home community, it strengthens our workforce and helps our rural communities thrive,” Rhoden said.

Applications will be accepted through April 15, according to the state Department of Education. The program will start in July.

As of December, 72 paraprofessionals had graduated from the program, and 240 had participated. Of the graduates, 59 signed teaching contracts — all at South Dakota public and private schools. Five graduates earned degrees in secondary education, 17 in special education, 17 in elementary and special education, and 33 in elementary education.

Stacie Becker graduated from the program’s first cohort.

“This experience showed me that I have the skills, perseverance and passion to become an effective educator,” Becker said in a news release, “and that with commitment, I can achieve my goals.”

Statewide, 144 teaching positions were unfilled as of July last year, weeks before the school year started, according to the Associated School Boards of South Dakota. That’s the lowest number in recent years: There were 202 unfilled positions in July 2024, 256 in 2023, 225 in 2022 and 174 in 2021.

The Department of Education started the program, but it is currently funded through the state Department of Labor and Regulation with a combination of state and federal grants to reimburse universities for the discounted portion of tuition and other programming. 

School districts pay $1,000 a year per apprentice. Apprentices are responsible for up to $1,000 a year in tuition, books and state assessments.

title: Spearfish discards fee removal  for rubble site branch disposal
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: For now, fees charged at the Spearfish restricted use (RUS) for branch disposal will remain in place. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson
_full_text:
Spearfish discards fee removal  for rubble site branch disposal
For now, fees charged at the Spearfish restricted use (RUS) for branch disposal will remain in place. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson 

SPEARFISH — For now, the fee collected when citizens drop branches off at the Spearfish Restricted Use Site (RUS) will remain in place, after a resolution to remove the charge was defeated on March 16 on a 7-1 vote.

Councilman Dean Myers asked that the council consider removing the fee, which is charged by the cubic yard, for residents dropping off branches after they trim trees. He was the sole member of the council who did not vote against keeping the fee in place.

“I think that we are going to tell people that they have to start trimming their trees up to, what, 10 feet or something to that effect,” Myers said. “Now we are going to charge them to dump those branches? I think this is a small concession to make.

Public Works Director Adam McMahon said that the RUS receives complaints about the charge for branches on a weekly and sometimes daily basis.

However, if the fee had been eliminated, he estimated the city might lose about $15,000 (or 10%) of the revenue at the RUS. 

“That is just an approximation. We don’t have a point of sale system at the restricted use site, so we are just approximating a portion of our overall revenue based on the volume of what we report to the state for what we receive,” McMahon said. “The other factor that I was going to add to this for consideration is if we are being asked to implement this change without a method to recoup that loss in revenue, or should we pursue recouping that loss in revenue through other means.”

Spearfish’s solid waste division, which runs the RUS, operates as an enterprise fund. That means it must bring in enough revenue to cover its own costs, according to county regulation.

“If we intend to continue to operate this as an enterprise fund, it should be generating the revenue needed to cover future costs. This year, I believe we are projecting a deficit of $47,000. That is even including the existing $13-15,000 of branch revenue. That deficit would increase,” DeNeui said. “There would come a point when you would have to change and implement another fee elsewhere.”

Speaking as a resident, instead of in her capacity as the finance officer, DeNeui said balancing the lost revenue with a city-wide fee might not be fair.

“From a personal perspective, because I do not use the rubble site, we have zero trees on our property now, I feel it’s fair that I don’t pay to dispose of any branches, because I do not have any branches on my property,” DeNeui said. “To have to pay a higher monthly solid waste fee for my trash collection to cover the cost for those who are using the rubble site, seems to me personally, as a resident, I don’t think that is fair.”

Officially, DeNeui said, she does not recommend eliminating any existing revenue stream in the city’s budget.

“I agree that not all citizens use the rubble site, so a separation does make sense. We really don’t want our garbage container costs subsidizing folks who don’t use the rubble site. My thought process has been that with the 2027 budget, it would be a good time for the cost versus revenue (analysis) of the rubble site because it should stand on its own. We should look at it globally. The garbage side of the business is subsidizing the rubble site,” said Councilman Larry Klarenbeek.

Spearfish resident Doug Markworth told the council he would like them to revisit the issue with more concrete data as well.

“We are going to make a decision based on a number we do not know … my recommendation is to table it until you know what you’re dealing with,” Markworth said.

Markworth said it may not be wise for the council to consider eliminating fees because they subsidize services that aren’t for everyone.

“It seems a little bit dangerous to me to start going down the path of, if I don’t use it, then I’m not going to pay. Somebody may not go to the opera house, but we had no problem putting $25,000, I think, over there,” Markworth said. In November, the city of Spearfish agreed to cover half the cost of repairing a stairway at the Matthews Opera House, in the form of a $35,500 matching grant.

“I don’t think we want to open up the can of worms, how many dollars every year come out of the city council and start using that as a standard for our community,” Markworth said.

When City Councilwoman Pam Jacobs made the motion to deny the fee waiver, she said she would like to see the issue reconsidered at budget season. Councilwoman Ana Rath said she would like to see a more consistent fee installed after a closer look at the RUS budget.

“I just want to reiterate, considering we do have 2 free weekends offered that people can save to unload their branches,” said City Councilwoman Jamie Hafner.

title: Slow down!
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Road signs exist for a reason, Meade County Commissioners said Tuesday.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — Road signs exist for a reason, Meade County Commissioners said Tuesday. 

Commissioner Rich Liggett reported on Tuesday that there have been multiple complaints about Haines Avenue, in an area where the speed limit is lowered to 35 miles per hour. That part of the road is in particular disrepair, which is the reason for the speed limit change, he said. 

“I have watched that area and people are not slowing down to 35 through that area,” Liggett said. “As a county, we have 1,100 miles of roadway, and it’s really frustrating to see people who complain about issues but don’t listen to the signs.” 

Meade County has a $4 million contract to reconstruct part of Haines Avenue this year, from the Meade/Pennington County line to Elk Creek Road. A full reconstruction of the road would cost the county about $15 million, Liggett said. With 1,100 miles of roads, maintenance is one of the parts of the county budget that commissioners struggle with regularly.

title: DCI investigating  officer involved shooting in Box Elder
byline: Pioneer Staff Reports
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
DCI investigating  officer involved shooting in Box Elder
Courtesy photo 

PIERRE — South Dakota Attorney General Marty Jackley announced the Division of Criminal Investigation (DCI), at the request of the South Dakota Highway Patrol, is investigating an officer involved shooting that occurred Saturday in Box Elder.

“The Attorney General’s Office and DCI approach every officerinvolved shooting with the utmost seriousness, and DCI will carry out a complete and unbiased investigation into this incident,” said Jackley. “We will release our findings once our work is finished. There is no continuing danger to the public.”

Saturday’s incident began when the Meade County Sheriff’s Office allegedly initiated a pursuit with a vehicle. The Highway Patrol located the vehicle in Box Elder. After a short pursuit, the vehicle was stopped, and the shooting incident occurred.

The troopers fired at the suspect, who sustained nonlifethreatening injuries. No one else was injured. The suspect’s name will be released at a later time.

In its investigation, DCI will process the crime scene, conduct a forensic examination of all collected evidence, interview those involved, and review all available video. DCI will issue a summary once the investigation is completed. The Box Elder Police Department, the Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office are assisting DCI.

The Highway Patrol is cooperating in the investigation.

This is the first officer involved shooting in South Dakota this year.

title: SD lawmakers test their skills behind the wheel at emergency vehicle driving course
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: A driver navigates a decommissioned law enforcement vehicle through an emergency vehicle operation training course on March 11 in Pierre.
_full_text:
SD lawmakers test their skills behind the wheel at emergency vehicle driving course
A driver navigates a decommissioned law enforcement vehicle through an emergency vehicle operation training course on March 11 in Pierre.  

PIERRE — After weeks spent split on property tax relief, economic development and other policy decisions, some state legislators seized an opportunity to blow off steam at a lawmakers’ day taking place just north of the capital city.

The South Dakota Highway Patrol and Department of Public Safety hosted about a dozen lawmakers at the Emergency Vehicle Operation Course.

Highway Patrol troopers, police officers and sheriff’s deputies, among other first responders, must be successful in the course that requires high- and low-speed vehicle maneuvering, driving in reverse and disabling other vehicles. That’s according to South Dakota Highway Patrol Lt. Dave Campbell.

For most of the participants, it’s a weeklong experience.

“They have a classroom section, and then a testing session where they have to perform every exercise,” Campbell said.

Law enforcement training classes are the largest, Campbell said, often with more than 20 students. Instructors get certified in Washington state, where Campbell said he gets ideas for new ways to challenge students.

The cars on the course are old Highway Patrol vehicles. Some of them are outfitted with metal bumpers as protection so the cars don’t get damaged beyond repair.

Madison Republican Rep. Tim Walburg is one of the lawmakers who came to test his skills on the course during the final week of the legislative session. He’s a former Lake County sheriff and currently works as a paramedic. Walburg hasn’t done a course like this one since the early 2000s — “a hot minute,” he said.

“This has changed a little bit,” Walburg added.

The course has speed sensors with lights to point drivers in one direction or the other at the last second to test reactions. Those light-up arrows only appear if a driver is going fast enough to trigger them. Walburg said when he was getting certified, he relied on an instructor to call out a direction to turn the vehicle.

The course was recently resurfaced thanks to a $2.4 million appropriation from lawmakers in 2021, Campbell said. Legislator days like this one help build relationships between law enforcement trainers and lawmakers.

“A lot of them hadn’t seen what their blessing went towards,” Campbell said. “And then they come out and they see it, they have fun, and they’re like, ‘What else do you guys need? What’s a future need that you guys might have?’”

title: Black Hills Community Bank and FHLB  Des Moines awards $700K to Spearfish,  Rapid City non-profits
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Black Hills Community Bank and Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines announce the award of $700,000 in grants to Spearfish and Rapid City non-profits. Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Black Hills Community Bank and FHLB  Des Moines awards $700K to Spearfish,  Rapid City non-profits
Black Hills Community Bank and Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines announce the award of $700,000 in grants to Spearfish and Rapid City non-profits. Courtesy photo 

SPEARFISH — Black Hills Community Bank, in partnership with Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines (FHLB), is pleased to announce that several non-profit organizations in Rapid City and Spearfish will receive a total of $700,000, through a member impact fund. The program provides matching grants to support affordable housing and community development. FHLB awarded more than $12.9 million across 5 states and US territories, including South Dakota. 

“Black Hills Community Bank is committed to investing in Rapid City and Spearfish, and we are proud to support nonprofits that strengthen our communities,” said Jack Lynass, CEO and President of Black Hills Community Bank. “Supporting our communities is a cornerstone of who we are. We are grateful to FHLB Des Moines for helping us invest $700,000 this year to the communities we are privileged to serve.” 

The $700,000 includes $200,000 in direct donations from Black Hills Community Bank, matched at a 2.5:1 ratio by FHLB for an additional $500,000. FHLB supports more than 1,200 members with funding for mortgage lending, economic development and affordable housing. 

“The member impact fund is designed to help our members champion the organizations that make a real difference in their communities,” said Kris Williams, president and CEO of FHLB Des Moines. “By partnering with our member banks, such as Black Hills Community Bank, we’re able to multiply resources and support their vital work as they continue to strengthen opportunities for the people they serve.”

Recipients were selected based on their need for funding to increase capacity or provide working capital for programs supporting affordable housing and community development, including job training, financial literacy, food banks and youth programs.Pioneer staff reports

title: Black Hills Pioneer at 150 Years: County Affairs
description: Pioneer file graphic
_full_text:
Black Hills Pioneer at 150 years
Pioneer file graphic 

BH Pioneer at 150
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

~ January 6, 1886 • Black Hills Daily Times ~

County Affairs.

Rumored Confession of Colonel Tracy,

Implicating a number of well-known citizens.

All sorts of rumors prevailed yesterday relative to irregularities in county affairs, as divulged by Colonel Tracy, who, it was announced, had made a statement to District Attorney Carey, when, how and to what extent the county had been victimized, also giving the names of his confederates. A TIMES reporter interviewed the District Attorney with very unsatisfactory results, for while that official would deny nothing neither would he confirm any of the reports. He said: “I am very sorry, indeed, that these reports got out, for they seriously interfere with my plans. I can give you no information whatever, unless you consider the statement of value, that the rumors, as I hear them, are not absolutely correct.”

Barney Canifield, attorney for Col. Tracy, was also visited and received his first inkling of the rumors from the reporter. He ridiculed the idea of a confession, for the very good reason, he said, that the colonel had nothing to “confess.” Despite these assurances the public is disposed to believe that a disclosure of greater of less importance has been made, and that surprising developments will shortly occur. The confession, it is said, involves a number of well-known citizens, the maximum, four, given out early in the day, increasing to eight by evening, and including ex-officials, present officials, residents of various localities, and indirectly pointing to a number of others not named.

A TIMES reporter interviewed several of those directly specified, each and all of whom treated the matter indifferently, if not as a very good joke. The grand jury meets today, when an investigation will be inaugurated. It is to be hoped that the work will not be alighted, and that the truth will be established no matter who may be hurt.

Expert Phillips continues at work with very good results. Indirectly we learn that an apparent if not actual fraud amounting in the aggregate to upwards of $15,000 has been established.

One of the latest revelations, but whether or not the result of Mr. Phillip’s investigation we are unable to state, brings in the Bob Neill settlement. It will be remembered that the bondsmen of that official deposited among other values, a quantity of county, warrants to make good the deficiency in the treasurers account. Those were placed, with all papers in the case, in the vault connected with they county clerk’s office. It is now alleged that on or about the date of maturity for such paper, the warrants were abstracted and their place supplied by warrants of equal face value in the aggregate, but of more recent issue, the object being to realize by immediate redemption and accrued interest. How much was realized by such transfer may be inferred from the fact that the old warrants were worth about $1.14, while those substituted had a market value of 65 or 70 cents. The names of the parties making the exchange are freely mentioned on the street in connection with the transaction.

~ February 20, 1886 • Black Hills Weekly Times ~

Si Minich.

A Former Black Hiller Hung at Leadville.

The Leadville Chronicle of Feb. 5th brings an exhaustive account of the hanging of one Cyrus Minich for the murder, October 13, 1884, near Leadville, of Samuel Baldwin, a teamster. The event possesses local interest from the fact that the culprit formerly sojourned in and around the Hills although little can be learned of him else than through certain meager confession made prior to execution, of which this is a sample:

A VERY MEAN JAIL.

Prof. Joab, one of a party of visitors remarked:

“This is a nice jail.”

“It’s the meanest jail I was ever in except one, and I had to dig out of that it was so mean,” said Minich.

Prof. J. —“Where was that?”

Minich—“Oh, you needn’t think I had to do it. It was in the Black Hills, in Deadwood. I had no particular reason for digging out, but them fellers that robbed the ‘iron clad’ treasure coach of Wells, Fargo & Co., were in with me and they had very strong reasons for wanting to get on the turf again. We pulled the old board floor up and dug out in one night. It was not no jail to speak of.”

Inquiry among officials present and past, fails to sustain the reported escape, still as several deliveries of the character claimed have occurred, Minich, under another name may have been one of the number. His correct knowledge of the character of the jail in a measure sustains his assertion.

FOR WHAT HUNG.

The crime for which Minich suffered was committed for mercenary purposes. Baldwin, an honest, industrious old man, was trying to save up $1,200 to pay for his farm in St. Augustine, Illinois, and had accumulated $1,160, which, as his confidence in banks and their security had become unsettled by a succession of failures that had occurred, he carried in a belt about his waist. Minich and others, including a man named Herr, who, according to Minich, formerly lived in Deadwood, learned of Baldwin’s wealth, and that he carried it on his person, and waylaying, shot, killed and robbed their victim. Here is believed to be in the Big Horn country. Numerous arrests were made until the crime was firmly fixed upon Minich as principal, and he was speedily tried, convicted and executed. He, it is believed, was a member of the band of stage robbers infesting the Hills in early days, and is suspected of participation in the Cold Springs robbery, during which Campbell, the telegraph operator, was killed, and Gale Hill seriously wounded.

title: Meade busing contract amended,  Dakota Bus to take over
byline: By Brandon Woltkamp Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Harlow Bus and the Meade School Board amended their contract March 9 for the remainder of the 2025-2026 school year to allow routes R-3, R-5, and R-7 to be managed by Dakota Bus.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — Harlow Bus and the Meade School Board amended their contract March 9 for the remainder of the 2025-2026 school year to allow routes R-3, R-5, and R-7 to be managed by Dakota Bus. 

“I got more calls than I have ever gotten on a single issue,” said Board Member Brian Voight. 

Meade Superintendent Wayne Wormstadt said there have been staffing and maintenance issues with Harlow Bus. 

Wormstadt said Dakota Bus had previously taken 6 bus routes for Harlow with mutual consent due to the shortage of drivers from Harlow.

“We had 3 routes canceled with 15 minutes notice before the end of the school day,” said Wormstadt on March 9. 

“We have had to notify our parents of combined routes and other bus adjustments on a fairly regular basis,” said Wormstadt. 

“Next year, our bus contract will be serviced by Dakota Bus,” said Wormstadt. The Meade School Board approved the contract in February. 

Dakota Bus will service busing for the Meade School District starting July 1.

Repeated voicemails left with Harlow Bus were unreturned.

title: Spearfish strengthens relationship with Booth Society in new agreement
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: The city of Spearfish has approved an agreement that will reinforce its relationship with the Booth Society, and the D.C. Booth National Historic Fish Hatchery and Archives. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Spearfish strengthens relationship with Booth Society in new agreement
The city of Spearfish has approved an agreement that will reinforce its relationship with the Booth Society, and the D.C. Booth National Historic Fish Hatchery and Archives. Pioneer file photo 

SPEARFISH — An agreement signed this week between the city of Spearfish and the Booth Society, the non-profit that offers visitor services to guests of the D.C. Booth National Historic Fish Hatchery and Archives, will ensure the society can access city facilities and preserve the relationship between both groups for at least a decade. 

City Attorney David Knox described the agreement as “express(ing) and extend(ing) the ongoing relationship” that the city has with the Booth Society. 

The D.C. Booth National Historic Fish Hatchery and Archives brings in about 200,000 visitors per year. Each year, from April to November, volunteers for the Booth Society stay in the “volunteer village” section of the city campground at no charge. The agreement preserves that free use in writing.

“This is the updated agreement for use of the volunteer village at the campground by the volunteers with the Booth Society,” Knox said. “The agreement will be in place for the next 10 years. It does have an auto-renewal provision, but it will be 1 that triggers us every now and again to come in and take a look, and make sure that everything is good with us, with the city, and with the society on the agreement.” A city council member will continue to sit on the Booth Society board — a provision of the new document. 

The volunteer village isn’t the only city facility referenced in the agreement. 

The Snapper’s Club once belonged to the hatchery; it was used for aquaculture. When that purpose was served, the hatchery donated the venue to the city. 

“I’ve been at D.C. Booth for 18 years. My predecessor 12 before that … we used the Snapper’s Club without charge as long as we weren’t stepping on any scheduled events and as long as we didn’t incur costs to the city. We set tables ourselves, cleaned up after ourselves, we just sort of, we would call the city, come pick up the key,” Hatchery Superintendent Carlos Martinez told the city council in February. “Now, we are told we can’t use the venue. That is one of those institutional knowledge things that just gets forgotten about, but that we are hoping to rebuild.”

The city agreed, formally, to allow the Booth Society to use the Snapper’s Club free of charge. 

“Really I just want to thank primarily David (Knox) and Jamie (Hafner, Spearfish city councilwoman) for a lot of the conversations that we had. We went through several versions and we really came together on something that we could all agree on. It has been a process, so thank you and thank you for considering this,” said Carolyn Ferrell, the executive director of the D.C. Booth Society.

title: GF&P proposes boosting elk  population objective to 9K
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
GF&P proposes boosting elk  population objective to 9K
Pioneer file photo 

PIERRE — A draft elk action plan includes a population objective of up to 9,000, and officials will continue to take public comment until the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks Commission meeting in May. 

The objective is higher than the original plan to manage for 7,800 elk, which is what GF&P presented in January. John Kanta, terrestrial section chief said the change is the result of public comment the department received after presenting the draft elk management plan in January. The department received about 200 comments about the plan so far, and he said about 80% of those favored increasing population objectives. Some groups, including the wildlife federation, wanted to go up to 10,000 elk. 

The remaining 20% of comments opposing the population increase expressed concerns about chronic elk depredation issues on private land in the Black Hills. 

But there is also a lot of public land in the Black Hills, Kanta said, and wildlife management specialists have been actively assisting landowners with depredation. Population increases would be done in strategic areas, far away from units that have elk depredation issues due to large amounts of private land. Additionally, Kanta said the department would not go straight up to 9,000 elk, but increases would be much more gradual. 

“Quite frankly, we’re not going to be able to do that in a year, or maybe even two years,” he said. “It’s going to take awhile for us to get up there if we want to go that direction, and we certainly wouldn’t increase in those areas where we know we’ve had issues in the past.” 

Kanta said the GF&P has a very good understanding of the elk herd in the Black Hills, and while it will take several years to reach a population objective of 9,000, it is possible to bring the population down quickly. 

“This is a species we’ve studied extensively,” Kanta said. “We do an aerial survey every four years and modeling efforts that we do in between those aerial surveys. It’s very accurate and we have a really good handle on the number of elk in the Black Hills. The other thing we know form the past is that fi we want to, we can bring this elk herd down, and we can bring it down fast. Where it takes some time to grow the elk herd, if we want to go the other way we can issue the licenses and bring the herd down. That’s important. If we can be strategic about it, we’re able to address any of those depredation concerns and do it fast with hunters. Our intention would not be to go right to 9,000 if we choose to put this in play. The current proposal that you all made, that’s to manage for 7,800 elk. That’s what it would be for the next two years. We would want to, if we changed this objective, come back to you and say ‘let’s start building the herd.’ What’s important to us is to monitor and evaluate habitat conditions in the Black Hills, and along with depredation that will dictate where we would like to go with that elk population.” 

In addition to increases in population objectives, the draft elk management plan eliminates the hunter success threshold. The former plan established a 75% or higher success threshold as an objective. Under the new plan, the GF&P will focus more on age structure, managing for 60% or more of bulls that are 4-years old or older. 

Another adjustment included in the draft management plan is to change the number of archery tags that are allocated, from 25% to 30%. That change, Kanta said, adjusts for the demand for elk licenses. 

The GF&P will take comments about its elk management plan until its May 7-8 commission meeting.

title: State’s investment manager urges patience with underperformance, waiting for better opportunities
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — The people who manage the investments for the state of South Dakota’s retirement system have underperformed for the third year in a row, contributing to a 1.4 percentage-point gap — or a $2.75 billion shortfall — between their 10-year performance and the benchmark they try to meet over the long term.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — The people who manage the investments for the state of South Dakota’s retirement system have underperformed for the third year in a row, contributing to a 1.4 percentage-point gap — or a $2.75 billion shortfall — between their 10-year performance and the benchmark they try to meet over the long term.

The state Investment Council manages nearly $21 billion worth of total assets that fund the retirement system for public employees and also support the state budget, including health care and education trust funds. The council has eight voting members and a staff of 28.

The recent underperformance is by design, State Investment Officer Matt Clark told the legislative budget committee this session in Pierre.

“We’re fine. Just muddling along and being cautious for an extended period if we have to,” Clark said.

The council weighs its performance against a Capital Markets Benchmark, which is an index that can be used as a comparison to measure an investment portfolio’s results.

The state has been “significantly below” its benchmark as stock prices rose in recent years, largely inflated by artificial intelligence-led investments, Clark said. His “contrarian” approach pulls risk back when markets are high to protect against anticipated market downturns and outperform by buying stocks when they’re cheaper.

The state’s stock investments are currently set to the “minimum risk position,” Clark said. Some other investments in real estate and private equity have not yielded the best results, but are key to a more stable and diverse portfolio, Clark told lawmakers.

Eric Ollila, executive director of the South Dakota State Employees Organization, represents and advocates for current and retired state government employees. He isn’t concerned about the investments’ performance yet. The council is still beating its benchmark over the longer term of 20 years and beyond.

“You have to look at the long-term trends,” Ollila said. “How it has operated has proven itself over the years and deserves the benefit of the doubt.”

Ollila added that South Dakotans “vest our faith in our elected officials” and appointed members of the South Dakota Investment Council and the South Dakota Retirement System board.

“We put them in a place to get things done,” Ollila said. “If we are unhappy with how they’re performing, we can replace them.”

Market downturn will follow inflated AI investment, state investment officer says

Clark, who has been with the Investment Council since the 1980s, said South Dakota is “underweight” in the artificial intelligence boom.

Clark compared the current AI-driven stock market to the “dot-com bubble” of the late 1990s. The investment council started pulling back from the market in 1996 and waited until the bubble burst. The stock market has been in a similar position recently with some of the most expensive prices since the 20th century ended, Clark told lawmakers.

Clark told South Dakota Searchlight that investors historically get “overexcited” about technological innovations, including railroads, telephones, automobiles and airplanes.

“In each of those instances, they were overdone in terms of stock market investor enthusiasm,” Clark told Searchlight. “You end up overdoing it, at least in the short term.” 

Many companies struggled or went bankrupt after those bubbles popped, such as fiber optic companies after the dot-com bubble. He expects the same for artificial intelligence.

By minimizing risk ahead of a downturn, that means South Dakota’s investors can “sidestep most of the damage” and buy cheap stocks while other investors are recovering from their losses. 

“Maybe we’ll set a new record, and we’ll have to suffer another 2 to 3 years, but we still believe strongly that staying the course with a long-term oriented, disciplined approach makes sense,” Clark told lawmakers. “It has to matter when markets are extremely expensive.”

‘Mediocre’ returns sustain system in the long term, officials say

South Dakota has been on the defensive since 2019, when Clark’s team anticipated an economic downturn. The pandemic and subsequent federal involvement prolonged South Dakota’s risk-averse approach.

That approach has consequences for members of the state retirement system.

Retirees’ cost-of-living adjustment is supposed to equal inflation, up to 3.5%. The annual adjustments are typically decreased to “what we can afford” for all members if retirement investments underperform, the South Dakota Retirement System’s senior actuary, Doug Fiddler, said during a December meeting. That annual adjustment depends in part on the system’s investments meeting a long-term return assumption of 6.5%. The system’s investment return last year was 5.44%.

The adjustment is the main piece of the formula the state can toy with, since the system must use fixed contribution rates, according to state law. The adjustment is also dependent on member demographics, salary trends, retirements, disabilities and mortality, which “often have an equal or greater impact on liabilities” and the annual adjustment, state retirement system Executive Director Travis Almond said in an emailed statement.

Due to less-than-expected investment returns, high inflation, and other factors, the retirement system’s annual adjustments fell short of inflation each of the last five years.

Sioux Falls School Board Member Marc Murren taught in South Dakota for 42 years before retiring in 2017. His wife taught for 33 years in Canton before retiring a few years later. They’re both receiving benefits from the state retirement system.

Murren is familiar with annual benefit and salary increases that don’t meet inflation. South Dakota sometimes does not increase state education funding to match inflation. 

“It’s like I got beat up when I was a teacher and now I’m getting beat up again because the retirement system isn’t doing its job,” Murren said.

Murren coached wrestling and football during his time at Washington High School, taught summer school and painted homes during the summers to earn more cash. He said he and his wife are “doing fine” in retirement, but he wants the retirement system to be more transparent in its communications about why benefit adjustments are lagging inflation. 

Almond said the system is “highly transparent” about how the adjustment is determined and when it falls below inflation. He said members of the public can attend board meetings in person or virtually and that information on the adjustment is sent to benefit recipients in the December newsletter, posted on the system’s website, and included in annual reports to the Legislature and governor.

The balance between cost of living adjustments, investment performance and other factors sustains the fund in the long term, Almond said. Without the “flexibility” to change the adjustment amount each year “it would be incredibly difficult to provide adequate retirement income.”

“The current structure ensures that retirees receive the largest COLA SDRS can sustainably afford year after year,” Almond said. “It prevents sudden funding shortfalls, protects the system’s long-term health, and helps ensure retirees receive their earned benefits for life.”

Clark prefers “disappointing but moderate” returns around 5-6% compared to risky investments that could lead to “challenging underfunding” of the state’s retirement system if the bubble pops.

“It’s no fun saying that the markets are high and bad times are coming and you have to be cautious,” Clark told South Dakota Searchlight. “We’re either going to get mediocre returns or worse, and we’re hoping for mediocre. But if there is a crash, hopefully we can take advantage of the volatility like we have in the past.”

Clark believes the state could “remain patient for an extended amount of time” until more attractive investment opportunities come along.

“We understand we’re investing people’s retirement money,” Clark said. “We have to be mindful that we invest in assets that are prudent and help us pay benefits people can depend on.”

title: BHSU students and staff win big  at AAF-Black Hills ADDY Awards
description: Black Hills State University students and staff received 47 Gold and Silver ADDYs at the 2026 American Advertising Federation Black Hills (AAF Black Hills) Awards Gala Feb. 27. Pictured from left are: BHSU President Steve Elliott; Emily Ashley, project manager at Fresh Produce; Heidi Marsh, account executive at Fresh Produce; Becca Walters, senior director of marketing & communications at BHSU; Maegan Detlefs, assistant director of marketing & communications at BHSU; Lori DuBry, print services coordinator at BHSU; and Scott H. Thornburg, media production coordinator at BHSU. Photo courtesy of AAF-Black Hills
_full_text:
BHSU students and staff win big  at AAF-Black Hills ADDY Awards
Black Hills State University students and staff received 47 Gold and Silver ADDYs at the 2026 American Advertising Federation Black Hills (AAF Black Hills) Awards Gala Feb. 27. Pictured from left are: BHSU President Steve Elliott; Emily Ashley, project manager at Fresh Produce; Heidi Marsh, account executive at Fresh Produce; Becca Walters, senior director of marketing & communications at BHSU; Maegan Detlefs, assistant director of marketing & communications at BHSU; Lori DuBry, print services coordinator at BHSU; and Scott H. Thornburg, media production coordinator at BHSU. Photo courtesy of AAF-Black Hills 

SPEARFISH — Black Hills State University students and staff received 47 Gold and Silver ADDYs at the 2026 American Advertising Federation Black Hills (AAF Black Hills) Awards Gala Feb. 27. Among the winners, the BHSU student editorial team of PONDER Magazine took home top honors for the second year in a row, with their third issue named “Best of Show,” the highest accolade a student entry can receive. 

BHSU students earning Gold ADDYs included Jenna Bondegard, Alec Butterfield, Josh Hall, Rae Hanzlik, Quill Lent, Shyanne Potthoff, Cole Marquez, Caddis Robinson, Noah Westergaard, and the PONDER editorial teams for issues 3 and 4. 

Silver ADDY recipients included Jaydn Anderson, Amber Aymar, Butterfield, Bondegard, Torrance Brown, Zakary Cina, Hunter Davis, Ava Elliott, Nathan Feller, Atlas Goodall, Hall, Sierra Hopp, Grant C. Koenig, Brooke Lammle, Lent, Marquez, Jesse Powers, Robinson, Taylor SanFilippo, Westergaard, and Julia Wilson.

In addition to their Gold & Silver ADDYs, three BHSU students received a “Judge’s Choice” award. This included Marquez for HOMIES Sock Packaging, Potthoff for “Breast Cancer Kills” Brand Identity Campaign, and Robinson for “Living on the Fly” Book Design.

In the professional awards category, the BHSU Office of Marketing & Communications, alongside their ad agency Fresh Produce, received three Gold and one Silver ADDY. Their BHSU-Rapid City campaign “Your Home Campus” also received a “Judge’s Choice” award, one of the top honors of the night.

“This year’s ADDY Awards highlight the remarkable creativity and dedication of both our students and staff,” said Becca Walters, senior director of marketing & communications at BHSU. “From our students earning top honors to our marketing and communications staff being recognized alongside Fresh Produce, these achievements reflect the collaborative spirit and high standards that define BHSU. We are immensely proud of the talent within our campus community and excited to see the work celebrated.”

All Gold ADDY winners will be entered into the American Advertising Awards District 8 competition which will include entries from Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota, and Wisconsin.

title: Sturgis students win safety award  at culinary arts contest
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — For Sturgis Brown High School students, safety was the key to sweet victory at the South Dakota ProStart Invitational Culinary Challenge.
_full_text:
STURGIS — For Sturgis Brown High School students, safety was the key to sweet victory at the South Dakota ProStart Invitational Culinary Challenge. 

Students in Mica Sulzbach-Bataille’s ProStart Culinary Arts class recently took top honors with the first Clark Kelley Legacy Award, which is given to a culinary arts team that demonstrates exceptional safety and sanitation practices during the competition. For instructor Sulzbach-Bataille, the award was the top honor students could achieve in a class that is all about teaching food safety and proper food handling techniques in the kitchen. 

“I was proud Sulzbach-Bataille said. “I cried. I think that’s a bigger award than even the cake and culinary. You have to have safety and sanitation in the kitchen. The judges said that in the first 15 minutes of them cooking, they knew that they were the winners. Clark Kelly was a pioneer for safety in the kitchen. 

Held March 9-10 in Pierre, the ProStart Invitational Culinary Challenge, put students’ culinary and hospitality management skills to the test, with 10 schools competing for awards in culinary, management, and cake decorating competitions. Representing Sturgis for the culinary competition were Kurtis Capp, who prepared a bacon-wrapped tenderloin steak; Ashlyn Larson, who prepared puff pastry apple roses with a caramelized cream cheese filling; and Xandria Goodall, who was in charge of the boiled potatoes, bacon-wrapped asparagus, and stuffed mushrooms. 

“My biggest challenge was juggling three different things at once, to figure out what needs to go where,” Goodall said. 

The biggest challenge for Larson was making sure to get her knife cuts just right, and figuring out the perfect time and temperature combination for her apple roses. 

“The apple roses ended up turning out beautiful on the outside, but the inside was still really raw,” she said. “It took a long time to figure out where the perfect temperature was and where to put it in the oven.”

Capp said his challenge was prepping ahead of time to make his steak. He’s happy with the team’s performance, but upset that he overlooked removing a toothpick that attached the bacon to the steak. 

Destiny Plunkett and Destiny Ownbey competed in the cake decorating competition, and made a chocolate coffee cake with vanilla swirls, caramel fudge filling, and Amish buttercream frosting. The buttercream, Plunkett said, was one of her biggest challenges. 

“For the buttercream, the butter kept separating from the sugar, so we had to whip it while we were on the floor,” she said. “It still ended up being a little to hard in some places, and a little too soft on other places, so it kind of dripped down our cake a little bit. We just tried to cover that with our fondant pieces, which I think mostly fixed the issue. The buttercream was really temperamental. If it was too cold, it would seize up. If it was too hot, it would start melting.” 

During the competition, students were given two butane burners, with an oven placed over one burner, two tables and a rolling rack. Students were not allowed to use electricity. For tue duration of their 1-hour time limit, the students were not allowd to step out of a 10 foot by 10 foot square. 

The many physical constraints forced the students to get creative with their cooking skills for the competition. 

“She had to melt chocolate, so we did a double burner on top of my boiling potatoes,” Goodall said. 

Larson said she was very proud of how her team overcame the challenges. 

“For culinary, I was really proud of us because throughout all of our practicing, we all had little mini struggles,” she said. “No matter what, I think we really did amazing with both the dishes. I was really, really proud of getting the sanitation and safety award, because I agree with Chef Mica. I do think it’s the biggest thing, especially in a kitchen. I think culinary did good. I think cake did amazing.” 

Goodall said the experience of the competition underscored the importance of teamwork in the kitchen. 

“For me the best thing was learning how to work in the stressful situation of everything, and the temporary fixes,” she said. “I honestly feel like this whole competition really shows teamwork. I think that was the greatest thing, too, because in a kitchen it doesn’t work without teamwork. So, I think just being able to work with people was my favorite thing.” 

ProStart Culinary Arts is a 2-year high school program that develops students into restaurant and hospitality leaders. The South Dakota ProStart Invitational seeks to bring the class curriculum together with real-world experience.

“ProStart is all about training them to be out in the restaurant (business),” Sulzbach-Bataille said.  “We learn about the front of the house, back of the house, what’s the protocol, what’s the proper way to talk in the kitchen. We try knife skills. They’re OSHA certified. They’re ServSafe certified. Then I teach them as much as I can cooking wise.” 

In addition to Sturgis, other schools that participated in the ProStart Invitational included Florence, Harrisburg, Huron, Mitchell CTE, Madison Central, Rapid City Stevens, St. Francis Indian School, Sioux Falls CTE, TF Riggs High School in Pierre, Vermillion, and Wagner. Top honors for the competition this year went to Mitchelle CTE, Harrisburg High School, and TF Riggs High School.
