title: Farm revenues fall as war and market concerns rise
byline: By Bart Pfankuch South Dakota News Watch
description: Grain farmer Jeff Thompson on March 20, on the farm he and his wife run near Lyons, S.D. Photo by Bart Pfankuch, South Dakota News Watch
_full_text:
Farm revenues fall as war and market concerns rise.jpeg
Grain farmer Jeff Thompson on March 20, on the farm he and his wife run near Lyons, S.D. Photo by Bart Pfankuch, South Dakota News Watch 

LYONS — Farmer Jeff Thompson had waited months for soybean prices to rebound, and he was getting a little antsy.

Like many of the roughly 18,000 other crop farmers in South Dakota, Thompson is storing corn and soybeans in giant grain bins on his farm because he can’t sell at a profitable price due to a variety of unfavorable market conditions.

After several months of waiting – and suddenly seeking an infusion of cash – Thompson made the decision in mid-March to sell off about 15,000 bushels of beans he had been storing since the fall 2025 harvest.

But the day he hoped to sell, a single social media post by President Donald Trump caused the soybean price to fall by 70 cents a bushel, a decline that would have caused Thompson to lose more than $10,000 on the sale.

Trump’s post indicated he would delay scheduled trade talks with President Xi Jinping of China, the world’s largest importer of soybeans and a country in which the U.S. is embroiled in a trade standoff that has hurt American soybean producers.

Thompson held onto his soybeans, and the wait for better prices began anew.

“The whole geopolitical thing keeps you awake at night and you don’t know what’s going to happen next,” Thompson, 64, said on a recent day at his farm in Lyons, an unincorporated hamlet located about 25 miles northwest of Sioux Falls. “Farming is risky enough on its own, fighting Mother Nature and all that, so I’m hoping things will settle down.”

A trifecta of terrible economic headwinds

The Trump tariffs and resulting trade wars that have devastated foreign export markets over the past year are not the only unsettling reality for South Dakota grain producers, who grew 1.1 billion bushels of corn and 238 million bushels of soybeans with a combined value of $6.4 billion in 2025, according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

The new U.S. war with Iran has led to the closure of a key global shipping route through the Strait of Hormuz, dramatically driving up prices for fuel and fertilizers. Farmers were already reeling from ongoing inflation that has raised prices for “inputs” required to grow crops, including seeds, water and electricity, rent or loan payments for land, and machinery and parts.

The trade war with China has led the Asian nation to forgo most orders for American soybeans and turn instead to South American producers. Prior to the trade war, about 30% of the soybeans grown annually in South Dakota were exported to China.

All that financial pain has been piled on top of stagnant low prices that have hurt revenues even as South Dakota grain farmers have produced record crops of corn and soybeans over the past couple years.

“The most difficult thing of all is that we’ve had 3 to 4 years of depressed prices,” Thompson said. “You can handle one low price year decently. But when they begin to piggyback on each other, it gets tough.”

Seeking new markets and uses

Since Trump took office and imposed tariffs on goods from other countries, cattle ranchers and other livestock producers have benefited from high sale prices and strong markets for their goods, as previously reported by News Watch.

But almost all other farmers in South Dakota and across the country are feeling the financial pain and emotional stress of an unfavorable global market for agricultural goods, said Scott VanderWal, president of the South Dakota Farm Bureau Federation who also serves as vice president of the American Farm Bureau Federation.

 Farm revenues fall as war and market concerns rise 2.jpeg
This Central Farmer’s Co-op grain elevator near Colton, S.D., is shown on March 20, with a large pile of corn that awaits possible shipment for export. Photo by Bart Pfankuch, South Dakota News Watch 

“By nature, farmers are pretty optimistic, but economically it’s pretty tough right now,” said VanderWal, a grain farmer from Volga. “The market right now is at a loss position and we’re about to plant the most expensive crop in history.”

National agricultural leaders are urging President Trump to find new international and domestic markets for agricultural products, VanderWal said. An ongoing hope is that Congress will approve year-round use of E-15, the gasoline that contains more ethanol and could boost corn prices and markets.

Jerry Schmitz, executive director of the South Dakota Soybean Association, was one of a dozen South Dakota officials and agricultural leaders who spent eight days in March on a trade mission to expand export opportunities in Japan and South Korea.

Schmitz said those two Asian countries — relatively small buyers of South Dakota corn and soybeans compared to China — were welcoming and eager to maintain or expand imports of American grains and goods.

The majority of South Dakota grains sold as exports are used as hog and poultry feed while a smaller percentage is used for human consumption, he said.

But even the most optimistic agricultural experts and producers are concerned the worst is not over yet for American farmers, Schmitz said.

Pull quote
“The market right now is at a loss position and we’re about to plant the most expensive crop in history.” 
  — Scott VanderWal, President, SD Farm Bureau Federation 

“Things have been difficult, and possibly this could continue for another year or 2,” he said. “Farmers are pretty flexible, but when bad news happens 3 to 5 years in a row, and with all the things happening in the world, it gets concerning.”

Fewer profitable farms possible this year

While it may be hard for South Dakota farmers to believe, the Rushmore State has fared better than most of the nation during the recent agricultural economic downturn, said Nate Franzen, president of agricultural lending at First Dakota National Bank in Yankton.

In 2025, the state had only one farm bankruptcy that Franzen was aware of, and about 80% of grain producers made a profit, a higher rate of performance than the 65% he predicted.

Franzen, who has worked in farm lending for three decades, said the best year for farm profitability in South Dakota over the past 20 years was in 2012, when the ethanol market was raging and about 93% of state farmers made money.

In 2025, record yields by producers and a high level of diversification on individual farms put South Dakota in a good position. But 2026 may be more challenging, he said.

“Looking forward, it is troubling,” Franzen said. “We’ve been experiencing inflation on the expense side for the last few years. And this Iran war is not helping because fertilizer is spiking and fuel is spiking.”

Financial and emotional stress the norm

Meanwhile, many farm families are enduring stressful kitchen-table meetings about money and the future of their operations, VanderWal said.

The challenging economic conditions are hardest on young farmers without strong equity or savings, those who recently took over from retiring parents or those who are heavily leveraged with loans for land or equipment.

“We are hearing more stories of bankruptcies increasing, and we’re hearing more incidents of farmer suicides again,” he said. “Farmers and ranchers are very proud people and it’s hard for them to ask for help, so we’re encouraging people to pay attention to family members and neighbors and get help for them if they need it.”

Schmitz said most South Dakota grain farmers are sticking to their planting plans for 2026 even amid great market uncertainty.

He said some farmers may plant more cover crops instead of corn and soybeans to build soil health, and a few may diversify their operations with cattle, hogs or poultry to create new revenue streams and generate manure as a source of low-cost fertilizer. Others may take on new jobs off the farm or expand the hours they spend working outside of agriculture.

Impacts felt beyond South Dakota farms

From a broader perspective, the weakening farm economy is hurting not only farmers but also the communities where they live, VanderWal said.

“Agriculture is what drives the economy of this state and very much so in the small communities,” he said. “These issues could really come home to bite the small communities of South Dakota.”

Ultimately, continued income challenges for American producers could lead to farm consolidation and greater corporate ownership, which could threaten not only American family farms but also the nation’s ability to feed itself, VanderWal said.

“Food security is national security, and if we have to rely on other countries for our food supply, that’s a real problem,” he said.

Thompson, the Minnehaha County grain farmer, said he is hopeful that new trade deals brokered by President Trump will hold up and remain favorable over the long term. He has applied for some of a recent $12 billion farm bailout approved by the president, though he considers that stop-gap program only a “small Band-Aid.”

Thompson hopes that when he retires in a few years, the agricultural economy will be strong enough for someone local to buy his roughly 800 acres and continue to use it for farming.

But he’s no longer sure that will happen.

“We love what we do and look forward to passing things down that we’ve built up over the years,” he said. “There’s a lot of younger guys who love to farm, but it just doesn’t pay.”

title: Sturgis chamber honors  community members, businesses
byline: By Mark Watson Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — The Sturgis Area Chamber of Commerce hosted its 80th annual awards banquet Thursday night at the Loud American Roadhouse.
_full_text:
STURGIS — The Sturgis Area Chamber of Commerce hosted its 80th annual awards banquet Thursday night at the Loud American Roadhouse. 

This year’s banquet paid tribute to community members and business that help make Sturgis a better place for all as well as a tribute to America as the nation celebrates its 250th birthday on July 4 with a USO-style theme. The Ponderosa Band quintet entertained the audience throughout the evening.

 After a review of top headlines that brought Sturgis into the limelight in 2025 as well as look back at chamber programing, it was onto the awards.

This year’s recipient of the Bob Davis Award, formerly known as the Key City Award, which is the chamber’s top award, was Kevin Forrester. Forester, who resigned last week due to health reasons, was unable to attend the event. His family and friends accepted the award in his place.

As part of the award, the Davis family presented $1,000 to a charity of his choice, which was Black Hills Trails. Forrester founded the organization which was instrumental in developing trail systems around Sturgis and other areas.

The Customer Service Excellence Award went to Grocery Mart.

The Employees of the Year Award was presented to Gary Matthews and Terry Anderson with KBHB Radio.

Bear Butte Gardens received this year’s Employer of the Year Award.

Community Volunteer of the Year Award went to Cole Staigle, of Pain & Movement Solutions.

This year’s Community Pride Award was presented to Kathy Seymour.

The New Business of the Year Award went to Mindless Kreations.

Indian Motorcycle Sturgis was presented the Retail Business of the Year Award.

This year’s Hospitality Business of the Year Award went to the Sturgis Motorcycle Museum & Hall of Fame.

The Industrial/Trades Business of the Year Award was presented to Freakshow Customs.

The Service Business of the Year title was awarded to Key City Chiropractic & Acupuncture.

First National Bank received the Sponsor of the Year Award.

New this year, in honor of the country’s 250 birthday, were two awards presented to veterans and veterans’ organizations. 

The Outstanding Veteran of the Year was presented to William “Bill” Atyeo.

And the Veteran Support Business of the Year award went to the Sturgis American Legion Riders Chapter 33.

title: Belle Fouche Ward 1 city council candidates
description: John Fyffe
_full_text:
Belle Fouche Ward 1 city council candidates
John Fyffe 

Voters in Belle Fourche Ward 1 will head to the polls April 7 to vote on one city council seat. two people are running for 1.5-year term. The Black Hills Pioneer emailed questions to the candidates. We are publishing their responses verbatim although some answers may have been edited slightly for length without changing the context of their answer.

John Fyffe

Q: Name and what you do for a living? 

A: My name is John Fyffe. I am an estimator, project manager and the Safety Coordinator for a commercial, residential glass and glazing contractor in Rapid City. 

Q: What motivated you to run for this office? 

A: Rising taxes, city spending, the quality of our streets... We have giant signs for every park in town but we still have streets in my ward that are dirt. We have beautiful walking paths throughout town and streets in my ward with no sidewalks at all. Where we do have sidewalks, they are in need of repair. Ward 1 has been neglected in many ways for a long time and I’m ready to help change that. 

Q: Why do you think you are best qualified for this position? 

A: In my professional life I have handled everything from payroll, accounts receivable and payable, contracts, purchasing, training and building my own company from the ground up. In my personal life I have been married for 25 years and have five children. Professionally and privately, I have become very good at listening to what people want but also giving it to them straight. As The Good Lord reminds me all the time, sometimes the answer is no. 

Q: With regards to leadership, what other elected public or nonprofit offices have you held? 

A: This is my first foray into public service. With regards to leadership, I have been running men and work for the better part of the last 36 years. 

Q; How many local governmental meetings have you attended within the last year and during your candidacy? 

A: I have attended maybe five functions in the last month. 

Q: Do you believe local newspapers are important? Why or why not? 

A: Local news, like, local politics is profoundly American. The local newspapers are the last, best example of the way the press were envisioned when the first amendment to the constitution was written and debated. 

Q: What are the most important issues that need to be addressed in Belle Fourche? 

A: Spending, taxation and infrastructure, in that order. 

Q: How do you intend to solve these issues? 

A: I will be one of eight councilmembers. I have no grand illusions that my one voice will change the city overnight I will however work for the interest of Ward 1 constituents and the interest of Belle Fourche residents. 

Q: What do you believe is working for Belle Fourche, and how will you retain that progress? 

A: The people of Belle Fourche are engaged and paying attention. From shopkeepers to mechanics, bartenders, hairstylists, equipment operators and so on, the Belle Fourche community is always looking out for their neighbors and willing to volunteer their free time to help organize, raise money or collect signatures. I only hope to add a layer of accountability and a voice in city government for them. 

Q: What does transparency in government mean to you, and how do you plan to be transparent to your constituents with your decisions?

A: If elected, the people of Belle Fourche will have my phone and address. Call or come see me, stop me in Dakota Lumber or Lynn’s Dakotamart or wherever and tell me what you think. 

Q: What else do you want people to know about you? 

A: I am a father of five, three boys and two girls. I have five grandchildren, three girls and two boys. I am married to my best friend and we have four dogs, Frank, Gracie, Gilbert and Daisy. I’m a Denver Broncos fan since the late 70’s. I have an old hot rod Ford pickup my son and I got running after 20 +/- years. I am a hard worker. Above all else I believe that Jesus died for our sins and was raised on the third day. God bless all of you this past Holy week and this Easter Sunday. 

Wilma Faith Goehring

Wilma Faith Goehring did not return her candidate questionnaire before press time.

title: Federal funding for Lawrence County Emergency Management still delayed following government shutdown
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — Months after the longest government shutdown in American history, and a partial shutdown ongoing, Lawrence County still has not received federal funding from the Emergency Management Performance Grant, a grant that partially pays for the county’s emergency management salaries and benefits.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

DEADWOOD — Months after the longest government shutdown in American history, and a partial shutdown ongoing, Lawrence County still has not received federal funding from the Emergency Management Performance Grant, a grant that partially pays for the county’s emergency management salaries and benefits. 

Brenda McGruder, county auditor, said the county has transferred approximately $115,000 from the general fund into the emergency management fund to compensate for the delay of funding. She said her office recently received communications saying that the monies have been approved and the state auditor should be receiving money to distribute to counties across the state soon, but no clear deadline or amount of funding has been given.

The Emergency Management Performance Grant is funded by FEMA, one of the departments affected by the 43-day-long government shutdown last year. According to FEMA’s website, the grant provides emergency management agencies with resources to work towards the national goal of a “secure and resilient nation.” The funds work to support efforts to “build and sustain core capabilities across the prevention, protection, mitigation, response, and recovery mission areas.”

 McGruder said the county’s emergency management department has been utilizing the grant for at least 23 years. To receive funding, the department sends quarterly reports to the agency, which then qualifies them for grant monies to partially pay for their salaries and benefits. In 2024, McGruder said the department received payment for the first 3 quarters in November and the last quarter in December. The department has still not received any payment for all 4 quarters of 2025. 

McGruder said the transfer caused no hardship to the county and she believes that funding for 2026 should arrive on time.

title: Belle Fouche Ward 2 city council candidates
description: Voters in Belle Fourche Ward 2will head to the polls April 7 to vote on one city council seat. Four people are running for 2.5-year term. The Black Hills Pioneer emailed questions to the candidates. We are publishing their responses verbatim although some answers may have been edited slightly for length without changing the context of their answer.
_full_text:
Voters in Belle Fourche Ward 2will head to the polls April 7 to vote on one city council seat. Four people are running for 2.5-year term. The Black Hills Pioneer emailed questions to the candidates. We are publishing their responses verbatim although some answers may have been edited slightly for length without changing the context of their answer.

 Faith-Kari.jpeg
Faith Kari-Melanson 

Faith Kari-Melanson

Q: Name and What do you do for a living?

A: My name is Faith Kari-Melanson. I work as an operations and emergency dispatch coordinator for commercial facilities. 

Q: What motivated you to run for this office?

A: I want to be a voice for conservative spending, personal freedoms, and property rights. I feel as though these principles are being slowly eroded. 

Q: Why do you think you’re the best qualified for this position?

A: I am not afraid to stand up for what I believe in. I do not cower to the bullies that try to get their way by belittling or by force. I have common sense and a willingness to do the job.  

Q: With regard to leadership, what other elected public or nonprofit offices have you held? 

A: I am young, only 23, so I have not had the chance to hold many offices, but I was the president of my 4H club in High School and I was also on the Chrisitan Education board at my church. 

Q: How many local governmental meetings have you attended within the last year and during your candidacy?

A: I have watched all the meetings from the last 5 months online. 

Q: Do you believe local newspapers are important? Why or why not?

A: Yes, local newspapers are profoundly important. They function as a pillar of community life by ensuring government accountability and encouraging community engagement.

Q: What are the most important issues that need to be addressed in Belle Fourche?

A: Spending and Transparency.  Spending because, as with most government bodies, it has gotten way out of control. Yes, I understand prices for things have gone up. That means we must cut back and not frivolously spend. It is not “city money” it is taxpayers’ money. And we need to be good stewards of the people’s money. We should not just keep raising taxes to buy the things we want. Many of these things, like all the 14 park signs around town, would have been better for a community group to raise the money by donation, not spending taxpayer money on a big sign. 

Q: How do you intend to solve these issues?

A: I am not naïve enough to think that I can solve these issues on my own. It will take working with others who are willing to cut spending, others willing to say no to projects we don’t have the money for. Using the money the people have entrusted us with to fix existing things like roads while cutting spending on things we have no business spending it on.   

Q: What do you believe is working for Belle Fourche, and how will you retain that progress? 

A: I love the small, hometown feel of this community. The way the people come together, our local festivals and community centered outreaches, the generosity of this community and its never give-up spirit. The people are what is working here in Belle Fourche.  

Q: What does transparency in government mean to you, and how do you plan to be transparent to your constituents with your decisions?

A: Transparency in government means ensuring all decisions, financial data, and policy discussions are open, honest, and accessible to the public, fostering trust and accountability. As a city council member, I will ensure transparency by doing just that- being open and honest. I will also use social media and other platforms to explain the “why” behind decisions, rather than just the outcome, allowing constituents to understand my reasoning. I will remain open to answering any questions people may have. 

Q: What else do you want people to know about you? 

A: I am a homeowner, a wife, and a mother. I have loved growing up in this community and all it has to offer. I loved my time in 4H and Shooting Sports, going to fun events here, enjoying holidays and the fun ways our community celebrates. I want to ensure the success of a strong Belle Fourche, so it remains the safe, wonderful community for my children as it was for me. 

 Falina Selchert.jpg
Falina Selchert 

Falina Selchert

Q: Name and what you do for a living? 

A: Falina Selchert. I am a real estate advisor and entrepreneur serving the Northern Black Hills. I help clients navigate one of the biggest financial decisions of their lives while also supporting local businesses through marketing. My work keeps me closely connected to our community, local economy, and the people who call Belle Fourche home. I am proud to live, work, and raise my family here. 

Q: What motivated you to run for this office? 

A: I love Belle Fourche and want to protect what makes it special while planning for what comes next. I chose to run because I saw a need for someone who is available, engaged, and willing to ask hard questions. I am not a yes person just to move things along. I believe in thoughtful decisions, even when they take more time. I am not here for a title. I am here to serve, listen, and do what is right for our community long term. 

Q: Why do you think you are best qualified for this position? 

A: I bring real world business experience, strong communication skills, and a deep connection to this community. I work with people every day to solve problems and make decisions with financial impact. I am approachable, responsive, and not afraid to do the work. You will always know where I stand and that I am putting Belle Fourche first. 

Q: With regards to leadership, what other elected public or nonprofit offices have you held? 

A: I currently serve on the Belle Fourche Chamber of Commerce Board, Parks and Recreation Board, and Hometown Thursdays Board. I am also involved with Realtor for Kids. Within the Chamber, I serve on the marketing, events, and advocacy committees. These roles keep me actively involved in decisions that impact our local businesses, families, and community growth. 

Q:How many local governmental meetings have you attended within the last year and during your candidacy?

A: I make it a priority to stay engaged and informed. I attend most city council meetings and occasionally attend county commissioner meetings as well. In addition, I serve on multiple local boards, which require regular meetings and ongoing involvement in community decisions. Being present matters, and I am committed to showing up, listening, and understanding the issues that impact Belle Fourche. 

Q: Do you believe local newspapers are important? Why or why not? 

A: Yes. Local newspapers are important for keeping communities informed and connected. They provide a trusted source of information on local issues, events, and decisions that impact daily life. Strong local journalism helps keep leadership accountable and ensures residents have access to accurate information. 

Q: What are the most important issues that need to be addressed in Belle Fourche? 

A: Infrastructure is a top priority, including roads, sewer systems, and long term planning for growth. We also need to focus on responsible spending to avoid unnecessary increases in taxes and fees. Clear communication with residents is another key issue so people understand what is happening and why decisions are being made. 

Q: How do you intend to solve these issues? 

A: We need to be proactive instead of reactive. That means planning ahead, prioritizing projects, and making smart financial decisions now to avoid bigger costs later. I will ask questions, review spending carefully, and work with others to find practical solutions. Communication will also be key so residents stay informed and involved. 

Q: What do you believe is working for Belle Fourche, and how will you retain that progress? 

A: What is working in Belle Fourche is the people. We have a strong sense of community, local pride, and individuals who step up and get involved. That is something you cannot manufacture. To keep that momentum, we need to support local businesses, encourage community involvement, and make smart decisions that protect our small town values while allowing for thoughtful growth. I will focus on preserving what makes Belle Fourche feel like home while being intentional about how we move forward. 

Q: What does transparency in government mean to you, and how do you plan to be transparent to your constituents with your decisions? 

A: Transparency means clear communication, honesty, and accessibility. People should know what is being discussed, why decisions are made, and how it impacts them. I will be available, responsive, and share updates so residents are not left guessing. You deserve to be informed and receive information in an easily accessible manner. 

Q: What else do you want people to know about you? 

A: I am a wife, a mom of three boys, and someone who truly cares about this community. This was not a last minute decision. I had planned to run in 2027, but when this seat opened up, I chose to step in sooner because I was already preparing for this role. My phone is always on, and I am always willing to listen. I have the flexibility to make this a priority and the work ethic to follow through. I am here to serve Belle Fourche, not just hold a seat. 

 Geno Pesicka.jpg
Gene Pesicka 

Gene Pesicka

Q: Name and what you do for a living? 

A: My name is Gene Pesicka, but most people know me as Geno, or most recently as Willy Wonka. By day I am a technician at Carl’s Trailer Sales in Belle Fourche, but by night … I’m Batman. Or asleep. More often asleep. 

Q: What motivated you to run for this office?

A: At first it was after attending the almost Jerry Springer-esque council meetings in January. I figured if that’s the kind of decorum they present to the public at meetings I can handle that. Then it was my wife, friends, business owners, and people of the community (including 3 former council people and a previous mayor) who reached out to me once the position for Ward 2 opened and said its time for me to step up instead of just joking about running for council. Once my petition was finalized it slowly evolved into an insatiable desire to become Supreme Overlord of Belle Fourche. 

Q: Why do you think you are best qualified for this position?

A: I have lived in Belle Fourche for 16 years. I’ve been involved in a wide variety of organizations, most notably Belle Theater, youth soccer and baseball, and was Co-Owner/Pool Boy of ‘The Leaky Pot Café’ with my wife Mikayla. I also worked for the Public Works Division of the City of Belle Fourche. Through these entities I have spent countless hours with so many different community members, businesses owners, families, and kids that I feel I have a strong connection with a large variety of people to know what the people of Belle want this town to feel and look like. 

Q: With regards to leadership, what other elected public or nonprofit offices have you held?

A: It all started in 6th grade when I was named President of our schools D.A.R.E program, LOL. I served on many advisory boards when I was in college and served on the Belle Fourche Youth Soccer board for 3 years. I have been invited to serve on multiple boards in Belle Fourche over the years but declined as to continue to focus on being a leader of the troops, like coaching youth soccer and baseball, being an announcer for the same programs, owing a business, and being an actor and event organizer for BFACT, instead of on the governing body over them. 

Q: How many local governmental meetings have you attended within the last year and during your candidacy?

A: I have attended 5 meetings since the beginning of January with 3 since I announced my intent to run for Council. I just wish I had brought popcorn for everyone at the meetings in January. Those were reality TV worthy compared to the last 3 where no more than 10 people showed up for each. 

Q: Do you believe local newspapers are important? Why or why not?

A: I very much believe local newspapers are very important to communities. Before social media they were the fabric of any town. We all looked forward to hopefully cutting out a picture of ourselves from the newspaper. I actually graduated from Black Hills State University with a major in Computer Publishing and Graphic Design and a minor in speech/journalism in the early 00’s with an emphasis on newspaper layout and journalism. I have about 50 BHP newspapers and 20 BF Beacons with my picture in it in my ‘Tote of Life’ of awards and events I’ve been a part of. 

Q: What are the most important issues that need to be addressed in Belle Fourche?

A: Community adhesion. Yes we need our roads, water and sewer lines, and our landfill fixed. And the city is currently in the process of resolving all these issues. But to a man or woman, you will find that we are a community disconnected from each other. Our school and community functions are not as well attended as they used to be. And council meetings barely bring in more citizens than there are ward reps. A city cannot sustain, grow, or evolve unless the majority of citizens work together to do that. Just because you can’t afford to donate your money doesn’t mean your can’t afford to donate your time to help. 

Q: How do you intend to solve these issues?

A: The same way I have been doing it for 30 years, by being involved. I am still going to help organize events to help local and school organizations. I’ve recently been organizing a group of improv actors to work at school events. Being on city council just gives me a bigger voice to connect with those looking to get involved or find a group that makes them feel welcome. I have always tried to be a leader by example. But as the saying goes ‘You can lead a horse to water, but you can’t make it drink.’ I will work to lead people to ‘their water.’

Q: What do you believe is working for Belle Fourche, and how will you retain that progress?

A: You only have to go so far as reading the Belle Fourche Community Forum Pages on Facebook to see that we are a town who cares. We are just divided. But it doesn’t mean everyone hates each other. Even the angry comments, if you take the time to fully read them and interpret them, its people who want the city to be better than it has been. No city is perfect. But the ones that are happiest are the ones who have learned to set aside their differences and let each group promote themselves and support all of them as well. 

Q: What does transparency in government mean to you, and how do you plan to be transparent to your constituents with your decisions?

A: Be honest. I said this at the public forum but its almost like being a parent. The kids who grow up the most well rounded are the ones whose parents don’t hide the truth from them. Kids are curious, they will find out one way or another. That doesn’t mean you have to go into all the bloody details, but you can’t shy away from it either. Government should be open to what they are trying to do. Breaking down penny by penny of what is being spent is not what the people want, what they want is hearing where it is going. 

Q: What else do you want people to know about you?

A: I’m an Aquarius and Bohemian; I like long walks on the beach and staring at the stars in the bed of my truck in Spearfish Canyon. Also, I’m not really Batman.

Seriously though, I want to see Belle Fourche find its identity in todays world while still staying true to the roots that defined it for generations and the security a town in the Midwest provides. The world has changed more in the last 8 years than it did in the 20 years prior. We have great teachers, great local businesses, and a safe way of life.

 Ida Cazer.jpg
Ida Cazer 

Ida Cazer

Q: What motivated you to run for this office?

A: I am passionate about my city and I didn’t see anyone else stepping up, and Now there are 4 of us.  

Q: Why do you think you are best qualified for this position?

A: I would never say I am best qualified because every candidate has certain skills they can bring to the table. What I can say is that since I am retired I have the time to be able to talk to the people without having to rush to be somewhere. 

Q: With regards to leadership, what other elected public or nonprofit offices have you held?

A: I have never held an elected office.

Q: How many local governmental meetings have you attended within the last year and during your candidacy?

A: During my candidacy I have been to about 6. During the last year I would say about maybe 1 or 2 a month. 

Q: Do you believe local newspapers are important? Why or why not? 

A: Yes, newspapers are important, because not everyone has social media or gets their news that way. 

Q: What are the most important issues that need to be addressed in Belle Fourche?

A: There are alot of important issues, some more important to one person, while something totally different to another. I mean to one person it’s the streets, to another it’s parks, and yet another it’s taxes. I believe they are all important, and will listen to all of the issues people have.

Q: How do you intend to solve these issues?

A: These issues can not be solved by one person, all I can do is listen to the people and do the best I can. 

Q: What do you believe is working for Belle Fourche, and how will you retain that progress?

A: I don’t know much regarding the politics of Belle Fourche, that I can say what is working for Belle Fourche politically. That is why I am running. So that I can learn and bring a positive prospective and give a voice to the people. Non - politically what works for Belle Fourche is the kindness of the people and how we support one another in times of need. 

Q: What does transparency in government mean to you, and how do you plan to be transparent to your constituents with your decisions?

A: Transparency to me means being open and honest regarding the decisions I make for the city. As for how I plan to be transparent to the constituents I will be myself, open, honest, and willing to talk to people regarding the decisions I make. 

Q: What else do you want people to know about you?

A: I have lived in Belle Fourche for over 50 years. I am a warm, open, honest person who will listen to people regarding their concerns about our wonderful Belle Fourche. I am not afraid to voice my opinion when I feel things need to be said and done.

title: ‘What is the plan?’: Spearfish business owners, citizens push council for answers on failing Colo. Blvd chip seal
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Community members are concerned about the condition of Colorado Boulevard and Main Street. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Failing chip seal.JPG
Community members are concerned about the condition of Colorado Boulevard and Main Street. Pioneer file photo 

SPEARFISH— John Williams owns and operates two businesses in downtown Spearfish. Every day, before he opens Spearfish Brewing Company to the public, the staff have to sweep the outdoor seating area. Then, they have to use leaf blowers to clear dust and debris. Finally, the crew wipes down surfaces so that patrons can sit in the sunshine to enjoy a brew and nosh.

Hours later, the dust is back.

That dust became enough of a source of frustration for Williams that he approached the city council.

“Within an hour or 2 of us opening, there is more dust that is accumulating on the tables, and I have to literally apologize to customers, and explain that the project has failed miserably and accumulated all of the dust out here,” Williams said. “It’s been extremely frustrating and tedious to deal with. I was hoping maybe to get a little feedback on that, what the plan is moving forward.”

The failing chip seal on Colorado Boulevard from its intersection with Canyon Street, through the downtown core, and Main Street’s intersection with Jackson Boulevard has more than just Williams asking questions.

“I’m going to ask the direct question that John just asked: Would council or public works like to offer an update on what the plan is with the chip seal project, what we’re looking at?” Spearfish resident Nathan Hoogshagen asked during the council’s study session on Wednesday. “I think it would be nice to get that to the community. I’ve talked to 100 people who want to know what is going on.”

Public Works Director Adam McMahon said that his department is well aware of the public’s concern.

“We are still in negotiations with the contractor on a solution. We are also collaborating with DOT, some experts there … to be able to ensure that whatever the solution is that we move forward with, we are not experiencing an issue where we experience a second failure,” McMahon said. “I want to assure everyone here that we are factoring in feedback from the public and the council, at least to the extent we can and still be in compliance with the contract with the contractors.”

McMahon said, depending on how contract negotiations go, there could be repairs underway this summer.

“Currently, the contractor’s plan is to have that worked out and be performing those repairs in the month of June, which is currently what they are targeting, but again, we are still in negotiations with the contractor,” McMahon said. “I know mil and overlay have been requested numerous times; there are some supplements and processes that would have to be put into place to make that happen, but I do not have a complete set of information to be able to present to the council.”

In the meantime, Williams asked that the city consider a proactive approach to managing dust and debris that have resulted from the failing roadwork.

“I would just like to support any effort to get this fixed in a permanent way,” Williams said.

title: Council to consider terminating agreement with one TIF 8 developer
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council will look at terminating its agreement with Platinum Investments, LLC. one perspective developer in the newly minted Sawmill Tax Increment Finance District (TIF 8).
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council will look at terminating its agreement with Platinum Investments, LLC. one perspective developer in the newly minted Sawmill Tax Increment Finance District (TIF 8).

In August 2025, the city entered into a land purchase agreement and a TIF district development agreement with Platinum Investments, LLC. City Attorney David Knox said that despite the purchase agreement, a real estate transaction was never closed. Platinum Investments previously agreed to purchase 7.5 acres for $914,760 to develop commercial and retail space on a parcel situated with Colorado Boulevard to the north and Maitland Road to the east. The sale was not completed.

Now, Platinum Investments has asked to terminate both its purchase agreement and the TIF development agreement with the city.

Also in August, the council voted unanimously to approve a second purchase agreement for 11.06 acres of property in the same TIF district for $483,500, with Schipporeit Investments, LLC. The plan, last fall, was for Dakota Equipment to eventually move into that space.

TIF 8 was adopted by the city council on July 7 to cover the costs of redeveloping the former McLaughlin Sawmill site on Maitland Road

title: S.D. Parks and Wildlife Foundation raising funds for park projects
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: PIERRE — The South Dakota Parks and Wildlife Foundation is stepping up to build important facilities and infrastructure that aren’t at the top of the Game, Fish, and Parks priority lists.
_full_text:
S.D. Parks and Wildlife Foundation raising funds for park projects

PIERRE — The South Dakota Parks and Wildlife Foundation is stepping up to build important facilities and infrastructure that aren’t at the top of the Game, Fish, and Parks priority lists. 

The foundation is embarking on a $10 million campaign to build a variety of park projects that are included in the department’s 5-year plan, but that are not top priorities because they compete with preventative maintenance projects. So far, the Foundation has raised more than $500,000, and it plans to start work on certain projects this year. 

About 54% of the total $205,000 in projects scheduled to start this year are in western South Dakota. They include a Visitor Center with ADA access and an ADA Amphitheater in Custer State Park, for $60,000, and interpretive signage along the Mickelson Trail, for $50,000. 

Some other projects that are planned for the future include a welcome center on the western side of Custer State Park, and bridge repairs along the Mickelson Trail,

title: Black Hills Pioneer at 150: John E. Clark Killed
description: Pioneer file graphic
_full_text:
Black Hills Pioneer at 150 years
Pioneer file graphic 

Black Hills Pioneer
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

~ August 18, 1888 • Weekly Pioneer-Times ~

Homicide.

John E. Clark Killed By Frank Thulen Yesterday Morning.

The little town of Minnesela, the county seat of Butte County, has just been the scene of another tragedy; the chief actors in which were John E. Clark and Frank Thulen. The circumstances leading to the killing, as nearly as can be ascertained, were as follows: A little before 2 o’clock yesterday morning, Clark, who as of late had been drinking considerably, and who was sharing his room with Jimmy Murrin, waked his companion and stating he thought someone was “after his horses,” announced his intention of going out to investigate. Murrin replied that the horses were all right and endeavored to keep Clark at home. The latter persisted, however, and left the house. A few minutes later Mr. and Mrs. William Mitchell, who had been sitting up with a lady friend, quite ill at the time, passed Thulen’s blacksmith shop on their way home. A light was noticed, and thinking the fact queer, Mr. Mitchell went to Thulen’s residence, aroused and informed him someone was in his shop. Horse thieves have stolen a good deal of property at and around Minnesela lately. Thulen had a horse picketed on the hill, and a saddle in the shop. Naturally enough presuming he was about to be robbed, he picked up his double-barrel shotgun and ran to his shop. As he entered the front door, Clark ran out the back. Thulen followed, calling upon him to stop, and repeating the call several times. Clark paid no attention, but kept running at full speed, until finally Thulen leveled his gun, fired the charge—goose shot— entering the unfortunate man’s right side, and producing death in about thirty minutes. From the fact that the shot scattered from the shoulder to the hip, those familiar with firearms are of the opinion that the two men could not have been less than thirty yards apart when the shot was fired. The only words spoken by Clark after being wounded were uttered as Thulen approached him, when he said, “Frank, you’ve ruined me for life.”

“My god, Clark,” was Thulen’s reply, “why didn’t you answer me? I didn’t know it was you.” Thulen immediately afterwards looked for and found Sheriff Stevens, to whom he surrendered. Clark was carried to his home and died a few minutes after reaching there. What induced him to enter Thulen’s shop, what he intended doing there, if indeed he had any object at all, is a mystery. Probably he was not very clear in his own mind as to his intentions, and had little idea where he was or what he was doing, since as before stated, he was considerably under the influence of liquor.

Justice of the Peace and Acting Coroner G. L. Smith empanelled a jury consisting of John. S White, J. R. Kenett and John McClure. The testimony of Henry H. Mather, Thomas W. Butler, George Fuller and William Mitchell was heard, after which the jury returned the following verdict:

We, the undersigned coroner’s jury called to determine the cause of the death of John E. Clark, now lying dead before us, find from the evidence that the said John E. Clark came to his death from the wound of a shotgun in the hands of Frank Thulen of Minnesela, Dakota, this 16th day of August A. D., 1888. J. S. White, J. R. Kenett, John McClure.

The above facts were gathered substantially from William Miller and Jabez Spaulding, who came in during the day from Minnesela to procure Clark’s coffin.

About seven last evening Thulen arrived in custody of Sheriff Stevens, but acting under advice of his attorney, Mr. Henry Frawley, declined making a statement. The present grand jury will be asked to investigate the case at once, in order that a trial may be reached at as early a date as possible. For the present Thulen remains in charge of Mr. Stevens. What course of procedure will be adopted by the defense cannot be stated at present, though it is probable an effort will be made to have Thulen admitted to bail.

Few men in the Black Hills have a wider circle of friends and acquaintances than Frank Thulen. Coming in during the year 1876, he has resided here continuously since. For a number of years he pursued his trade, blacksmithing, in this city, occupying a shop near what is now the French hotel. In 1880 he was appointed on the police force, holding the position for two years, and proving himself one of the most efficient and conscientious officers ever in the employ of the municipality. In the spring of ’82 he resigned and moved to Minnesela, where he has since resided, always conducting himself in an upright, honorable way, and earning an enviable reputation. Though but little question exists his act was excusable by circumstances, and that the trial jury can not fail to take this view of it, nevertheless the affair is sad and unfortunate, and numerous friends will regret to learn of the occurrence.

title: BHSU theatre to perform  murder mystery dinner fundraiser
description: The cast of the BHSU Theatre Department rehearse for their upcoming murder mystery show “Murder’s in the Heir.” This fundraiser dinner performance will be April 11 in the Jacket Legacy Room at 5:30 p.m. BHSU Photo by Payton Sheehan
_full_text:
BHSU theatre to perform  murder mystery dinner fundraiser
The cast of the BHSU Theatre Department rehearse for their upcoming murder mystery show “Murder’s in the Heir.” This fundraiser dinner performance will be April 11 in the Jacket Legacy Room at 5:30 p.m. BHSU Photo by Payton Sheehan 

SPEARFISH – The Black Hills State University Theatre Department will host an interactive murder mystery dinner fundraiser April 11 in the Jacket Legacy Room, where guests will be asked to determine who killed tyrannical billionaire Simon Starkweather in a performance of “Murder’s in the Heir,” directed by BHSU student Josiah Sabrowski-Johnson. 

“This event isn’t just a show; it’s an experience. The audience becomes part of the story, which makes the single performance unique,” said student director Sabrowski-Johnson, who has also had a hand in other productions in the theatre department. “I believe this opportunity has been a real window into my future, as it is my aspiration to become a theatre instructor and director,” he added.  

The performance, written by Billy St. John, follows potential heirs who each have a personal motive to inherit Starkweather’s fortune for themselves. After Starkweather’s shocking murder, his grandson Simon III and detective Mike Davis are determined to find the perpetrator. 

“This audience-interactive murder mystery is going to be a blast! Not only will the audience have a wonderful three course meal, but they get to decide who the murderer is!” said Casey Hibbert, instructor of fine arts at BHSU. “Also, all the proceeds go to scholarships for the students to continue to do cool things like this.” 

The evening’s menu features a chipotle lime shrimp cocktail and mini beef wellington for starters, and a choice of chicken scallopini, with a chicken breast tenderloin, crispy prosciutto, fried capers, lemon and butter sauce, roasted garlic and parmesan mashed potatoes, and roasted broccolini as the main course, and a chocolate decadence with raspberry sauce for dessert. 

A vegan option of a stuffed portobello mushroom, roasted zucchini, red peppers, spinach, corn, quinoa, garlic roasted fingerling potatoes, and roasted broccolini is available as well. 

The event will also feature a cash bar that includes a special show-themed mocktail. 

Appetizers will be passed around at 5:30 p.m. and dinner will be served at 6:30 p.m. The performance will begin at 7 p.m. and dessert will be passed out during the intermission.  

“Not only is this production the start of many other student-directed shows here at BH, but it is also a fundraiser that will allow more students like me to attend this school,” said Sabrowski-Johnson. 

Tickets are $70 per person at BHSU.edu/BoxOffice. 

Find out more and purchase tickets at BHSU.edu/BoxOffice. For further questions, please contact Casey Hibbert at Casey.Hibbert@BHSU.edu.

title: SD Secretary of State releases guidance on new SD voter registration law
byline: By Mark Watson Black Hills Pioneer
description: PIERRE — While the SAVE act is being debated in the halls of Congress, a new similar voter registration law is now in effect in South Dakota.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

PIERRE — While the SAVE act is being debated in the halls of Congress, a new similar voter registration law is now in effect in South Dakota.

Secretary of State Monae Johnson issued guidance March 26 on SB 175, the new voter registration law that was signed by Gov. Larry Rhoden earlier in the day. Due to an emergency clause, SB 175 took effect immediately.

Johnson said for South Dakotans who are already registered to vote, no action is needed, according to the new law. Even if they need to update information on their voter registration form, such as address or party affiliation, no documentation is needed.

The new law only applies to people registering to vote for the first time or who have been removed from the state’s voter rolls.

Q: When is the June primary?

A: The primary election is June 2.

Q: When is the last day to register to vote?

A: May 18. South Dakota requires all registrations to be complete 15 days prior to the election.

Q: What documents are needed to register to vote now?

A: According to Johnson’s guidance, SB 175 requires that a person registering to vote for the first time or who has been removed from the state’s voter rolls provide documentary proof of citizenship. Proof of citizenship can be any one of the following documents:

• A South Dakota driver license or nondriver identification card that was issued after July 1, 2025, and indicates citizenship status on the back of the card;

• Another state’s driver license or nondriver identification card that indicates the person has provided sufficient documentation to demonstrate citizenship;

• A tribal identification card; or A legible photocopy of:

• A birth certificate

• A United States passport

• A consular report of birth abroad issued by the United States Department of State

• A certificate of naturalization; or

• Any other type of acceptable documentary evidence permitted under 42 C.F.R. § 436.407.

Q: What if I am already registered and am updating information on my voter registration form?

A: Johnson wrote in her email, the law does not require individuals who are already registered to vote to provide proof of citizenship. If someone is already registered to vote but is updating their information, such as their address or political party affiliation, the law does not require them to present proof of citizenship. An individual who has changed their name, such as a newly married woman, does not need to provide proof of citizenship if they are already registered to vote.

Q: How can I find out if I am registered to vote?

A: Johnson said you can check your current registration status at: https://vip.sdsos.gov/VIPLogin.aspx. 

Although driver’s licenses with the star on the front are REAL-IDs, the government’s new standard form of identification, those issued before July 1, 2025, will not be accepted as the lone form of ID when registering to vote.

Q: So, what documents do I need to get an updated driver’s license?

A: According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety (DPS), citizens must provide one of the following documents. Photocopies are not accepted, and addresses cannot be handwritten. 

• U.S. Certified birth Certificate (must be issued by a State or County)

• Valid, unexpired U.S. Passport or U.S. Passport card

• Consular Report of Birth Abroad from the U.S. Department of State

• Certificate of Citizenship or Naturalization

Additionally, you must prove your Social Security Number. This can be done by providing one of the following documents:

• Social Security Card

• W-2 - must include name and full Social Security Number

• Form 1099 - must include name and full Social Security Number

• Paycheck stub - must include name and full Social Security Number

You must prove your address by providing 2 documents which must be dated within the last year and include:

• Your name

• Show your physical address

Examples of address documents include:

• Your utility bill

• A bank statement

• Mortgage or lease agreement

• Phone bill

• Your vehicle registration

• Proof of vehicle insurance

• Income tax form from employer

• A medical bill

• Credit card statement/bill

Q: What if I need to update my name on my driver’s license?

A: According to the DPS, citizens must provide one of the following documents.

• Court Order - Provide Certified Court Order showing name change(s)

• Certified Marriage Certificate

If you have had more than one name change, you will need provide one of the documents above to show each name changes from birth to present day. State officials must be able to follow the name progression from your birth certificate to the name you will use on your driver license/identification card.

Q: How do I request a birth certificate or marriage certificate?

A: If you were born or married in South Dakota, you can go to any county’s register of deeds office and request the form. You will need a photo ID, that is not expired. It costs $15 and takes about 15 minutes.

If you were born or married out of state, visit the state government’s website. A search to the appropriate department is generally easy to find. The fees and times it takes to receive the certified copies vary. For example:

• In Wyoming, it takes 3-5 business days plus mailing time to receive a document. 

• In Texas, it takes 25-30 days.

• In California, it takes 5-7 weeks.

While name changes, due to a marriage, are the most common reasons why people change their name, adults and minors alike can legally change their name through the court. So far this year, the Black Hills Pioneer has published legal notices regarding name changes 14 times. 

People changing their name following a marriage or divorce do not need to publish that information in the legal newspaper.

The Pioneer reached out to Lawrence County Auditor Brenda McGruder for her understanding of the new law and guidance provided by the secretary of state. County auditors run the elections in each county. Her understanding mirrored the secretary of state’s guidance. The Pioneer also asked her additional questions, not answered by the secretary of state’s guidance released March 26.

Q: What if I cannot provide the necessary documents when I register to vote?

A: McGruder said if a new voter marks yes, they are a US citizen but provides no proof, staff will mark them as a “Federal Voter” which results in the voter getting a federal ballot. 

• If a new voter marks yes, they are a US citizen, and provides proof of US citizenship the voter is entered, and they will get the full ballot.

• If a new voter marks no, they are not a US citizen, the voter will be marked as invalid and incomplete until US citizenship can be provided. 

Q: If someone believes they are registered to vote, they go to the polls and their name is not in the election book, what can they do?

A: McGruder said election workers are trained to reach out to the Auditor’s Office so we can get the problem resolved. If we cannot find where a person has registered to vote the voter is given the option of voting a provisional ballot.  

Q: If someone, not yet registered to vote, has a driver’s license issued before July 1, 2025, and has changed their name, what additional documents are needed?

A: McGruder said common sense would tell you an official examining the registration detail could look at the photo and match the face to the name; however, she did not believe this would be allowed by the new law. The secretary of state’s office did not provide that guidance to county auditors.

The Pioneer reached out to the secretary of state’s office March 26, the day the guidance was issued, with additional questions. Christine Lehrkamp, director of the division of elections, responded the following day asking for patience while the office staff was busy verifying election petitions.

“We are extremely busy this time of year.  And no, I cannot tell you when I will get you a response,” she wrote. 

On Monday, a second employee reached out stating he was gathering answers, but as of Thursday afternoon, he had not responded with answers to the Pioneer’s questions.

Reactions to the bill

The bill passed nearly unanimously in both the state house and senate. 

After Rhoden signed the bill, the South Dakota Democratic Party and ACLU spoke against it.

“There is no reason for this law. We already have a system in place to verify people’s citizenship, and it is working,” said SD Democratic Party Chair Shane Merrill. “There have been no instances of non-citizens voting in our elections. Instead, this law creates a 2-tiered system in our state, telling some South Dakotans that they aren’t good enough to vote.

Samantha Chapman, advocacy manager for ACLU of South Dakota, elaborated.

“Previously, you didn’t need to prove your citizenship status to be able to register as a voter, which is one of your fundamental rights as an American,” Chapman said. “Now, when you go to register to vote, you’ll have to present your birth certificate or your passport or some document that can show that you’re a US citizen before you’re able to register to vote. Most people don’t have that kind of documentation on their person when they’re out and about. So, if you wanted to stop by your auditor’s office on your lunch break to register to vote, and you turn up and you don’t have these documents, well, it turns out you can’t register to vote, or you’re registered as a federal only voter. So, what this bill, what this law, will now do is limit the number of people who would otherwise be eligible to vote in South Dakota from being able to register to vote because they don’t have the documentation necessary.”

The Pioneer questioned her on people not having access to documents such as a birth certificate.

“I think if you were to ask 10 random people on the street if they knew where their birth certificate was at a given moment, most people don’t know where it is,” she said. “More than 60% of South Dakotans don’t have a valid passport to be able to use to for voter registration to prove the citizenship. And additionally, people who are naturalized citizens, they only get one copy of their naturalization document. It’s unlikely they want to carry that around or take that to the auditor’s office to make a copy of it. It’s a pretty sacred document to a lot of those folks.” 

Q: Who will be the most at risk for not being able to provide the required documents?

A: “This is really going to hurt low-income South Dakotans, rural South Dakotans, especially young South Dakotans who are registering for the first time, maybe at their campus address, and they don’t have their birth certificate, maybe it is at home with Mom and Dad, and since there’s an emergency clause here, there’s not much time to get the word out to the public, to educate and inform our election administrators before the June primary voter registration cutoff date,” Chapman said.

How they voted

In District 31, legislators were split on their support of the bill. Sen. Randy Deibert, R-Spearfish, voted against the bill, while Reps. Scott Odenbach, R-Spearfish, and Mary Fitzgerald, R-Spearfish, voted in favor.

Q: Why did you vote against the bill?

Deibert: “There were three reasons,” Deibert said. “One, the bill was poorly written. Two, it did not justify a emergency clause. And three, the paperwork for the bill was not ready to give the auditors proper instruction. They don’t have the forms. They don’t have instruction for this. So those were the three reasons I voted against it. So (March 26), I see they signed the bill, and the Secretary of State has given some indication to the auditors of how to handle it, but up until (March 26), there were no indications of how they were going to handle that, so the auditors didn’t know what to do with it if it did pass.”

Q: Are you OK with the requirements?

Deibert: “The issue I had was, it wasn’t clear whether the person had to have those documents when they changed address, change their name, change their location, or change their party affiliation. The bill was unclear about those changes,” Deibert said. “What the sponsor said was, ‘well, it’s very clear. It’s first time you register, you need that information.’ That’s not how the bill read. I don’t have a problem proving you’re a citizen. I don’t have a problem doing that. We’re doing that. It was more of how the bill was written and how it would be handled by the auditors that I was concerned with.”

Q: Why did you vote in favor of the bill?

Odenbach: “I voted for that in state affairs and voted for it on the floor,” Odenbach said. “I think it’s a no brainer. You need to be a citizen of this country to vote, and I thought the bill was a fine way to make sure that people can provide proof of that.”

Fitzgerald: “I think it’s to basically reduce the risk of any non-citizens voting, and whether that’s a real or perceived at this time, it’s just a way to, kind of to make sure that only those citizens are voting,” Fitzgerald said. “And one thing I like about it, is there some uniformity to that law? Now, it’s kind of like when you go to get your driver’s license, it’s kind of those same things that you need to prove if you’re going to register to vote, and if you’re already registered. So, I didn’t think there was any harm in it, in voting for it and implementing that so that maybe we can quiet some of those concerns that people have in regard to voter fraud and having non-citizens vote.

Q: How rampant is voter fraud in the United States?

A: The far-right leaning Heritage Foundation noted, since 2005, there were 7 cases of voter fraud in South Dakota. Most were from people voting more than once. None were because they were not citizens.

Nation-wide, according to the Heritage Foundation, since 1982, there have been 100 cases of “aliens” voting illegally. During that same general timeframe, since 1980, According to The American Presidency Project, there have been 1.41 billion votes cast just in presidential elections.

Q: Are you concerned that some people, elegible to vote, will not be able to provide the documents necessary register?

Odenbach: “No, I’m not concerned.”

Fitzgerald: “If they’re on any kind of assistance, whether that be Medicaid or SNAP or whatever, they’re going to have that verification in their file,” Fitzgerald said. “And I’ll just tell you, when I worked with Social Services, we would have people, let’s say, move to Deadwood or Belle Fourche, and they would come in and apply for assistance. Those folks had everything in a manila envelope. They had birth certificates, everything. They knew the procedure. I can say, working for Department of Social Services for 10 years, I don’t think that’s a problem. And if people want to be on some kind of assistance, we always help them. That was part of our job to make sure that they did get those verifications. So I’m not so worried about that, because I think there’s a lot of places they can go to get help.”

title: City council to consider authorizing a new affordable housing TIF in Spearfish
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: This map outlines the location of proposed TIF 9. If approved, developer Lloyd Companies would build multi-family housing that is eligible for reimbursement through the tax increment finance system. Map courtesy city of Spearfish
_full_text:
TIF map.png
This map outlines the location of proposed TIF 9. If approved, developer Lloyd Companies would build multi-family housing that is eligible for reimbursement through the tax increment finance system. Map courtesy city of Spearfish 

SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council will have the option of approving a request to establish a new tax increment finance district between Platinum Drive and Paramount Street, west of Culver’s and the Holiday Inn and Convention Center.

TIF 9, if established, would encourage the development of the roughly 28.6-acre parcel into “affordable” multi-family housing, Spearfish Community Development Director Peter Wysocki said.

If approved, a multi-family development on the site would have rental rates capped at 80% of Area Median Income (AMI) for at least 10 years. AMI is currently $102,200.

“The rents are capped based on the number of bedrooms in the unit. So, for example, a 2-bedroom unit cannot be rented for more than $1,840,” Wysocki said.

A rate cap, which restricts rent, is distinct from an income cap. Some affordable housing projects restrict the income of prospective renters to a certain percentage of AMI. That would not be the case with TIF 9, Wysocki said.

 TIF presentation alt.JPG
Spearfish Community Planning Director Peter Wysocki is pictured presenting an application for a new tax increment finance district (TIF) to the city council. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

“If we’re not using federal grants … there are no restrictions on income. It is likely that these units will be rented out to individuals making less than 80% of AMI, but the state requirement is to limit the rental rates,” Wysocki explained.

Tax increment financing is a public financing system that uses future increases in property taxes to reimburse the costs of public improvements built within a designated TIF boundary. The value of all the properties inside the district is assessed or calculated, and the total amount of property tax generated by all those properties is noted — the base amount of property tax revenues. All property revenue above this base amount, for the life of the TIF, is captured by the TIF to refund the costs of the public improvements or project. This amount — the amount over the base — is called the “tax increment.” Over time, property values are presumed to increase in part due to infrastructure improvements brought about by the TIF project itself. When the TIF is retired, the total taxation values revert to the original taxing entities.

In the case of the proposed TIF, TIF 9, the developer, Lloyd Companies, would be able to use the tax increment, the difference between the parcel’s current value and its value on completion, to cover the cost of purchasing the land, extending Paramount Drive, installing sanitary sewer, storm water, and water infrastructure and street lighting, and the construction of a multi-family housing development — $7.3 million in expenses. Wysocki said Lloyd Companies is only seeing $3.2 million in reimbursements via the TIF system. 

TIFs are allowed under state law, SDCL 11-9, to move projects that eliminate “blight” or implement the goals of a community’s comprehensive plan. 

Some of the definitions of “blight” for the purpose of understanding TIF districts are: an area that has defective or inadequate street lay outs, the substantial presence of deteriorated or substandard structures, insanitary or unsafe conditions, defective or unusual title conditions, or a “predominance of open space with obsolete platting, diversity of ownership, or deterioration of structures or site improvements.”

Wysocki pointed out that the project aligns with the city’s comprehensive plan, building up housing stock in the city.

When it comes to determining whether or not a TIF is an appropriate mechanism for encouraging development in the area, Wysocki said, the onus is on the city council. 

In his presentation Wysocki noted the “but for” test, that municipal bodies can use to determine if establishing a TIF makes sense. That test poses the question “Would a given project happen if it wasn’t for the TIF?”

Planning commission, on a 4-1 vote, recommended approval of the TIF already, finding that it met the TIF requirements in state statute.

title: SURF announces 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholars
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: The 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholars include Lewis Carlyle, Zachery Conway, Ellie Erbentraut, Quinn Kellar, Ethan Oolman, Emma Plamp, Sannah Windy Boy, and Joshua Wolfe. Courtesy photo
_full_text:
SURF announces 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholars
The 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholars include Lewis Carlyle, Zachery Conway, Ellie Erbentraut, Quinn Kellar, Ethan Oolman, Emma Plamp, Sannah Windy Boy, and Joshua Wolfe. Courtesy photo 

LEAD — The Institute for Underground Science at Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF) has announced recipients of the 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholarship.

The Davis-Bahcall Scholars program is designed to help South Dakota students who are considering a career in STEM to develop an understanding of where their passions can take them. The program demystifies STEM careers by exposing students to what professionals do every day. From June 13 through July 12, students will spend time exploring leading STEM industries at the Sanford Underground Research Facility, Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory in Illinois, and Boulby Underground Laboratory in the United Kingdom. 

“This program helps students imagine what a future in science could look like,” said Stacie Granum, program manager for The Institute for Underground Science at SURF. “By visiting world-class laboratories, meeting leading researchers, and experiencing real research environments, these students gain a firsthand look at discovery in action. Inspired by the legacy of Ray Davis Jr. and John Bahcall, the program encourages students to follow their curiosity and see themselves as part of the next generation of scientific discovery.”

The 2026 Davis-Bahcall Scholars include Ellie Erbentraut from Sioux Falls, who is attending the Massachusetts Institute of Technology; Emma Plamp, from Mitchell, who will graduate from Mitchell High School this year; Ethan Oolman from Sioux Falls, who is attending the University of South Dakota; Joshua Wolfe, from Flandreau, who will graduate from Flandreau Indian School this year; Lewis Carlyle, from Rapid City, who will graduate from Stevens High School this year; Sannah Windy Boy, from Worden, Mont., who is attending South Dakota School of Mines and Technology; Quinn Kellar from Mandan, N.D., who is attending Black Hills State University; and Zachery Conway, from Rapid City, who will graduate from Stevens High School this year.

Erbentraut said the program offers a chance to pursue her passion for physics that was first sparked in middle school, when an educator from SURF visited her classroom in Sioux Falls with hands-on learning exercises exploring dark matter. 

“I’m deeply interested in learning more about the day-to-day lives of scientists, so obtaining insight into the careers and facilities of leading researchers and academics would be invaluable. Knowing what a career in science could look like would inform my future ambitions. Touring world-class facilities and seeing with my own eyes potential workplaces would only refine my vision of what lies ahead for me,” said Erbentraut, who is studying electrical engineering and physics at MIT. 

Wolfe, hopes to leverage his fascination with science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) subjects into a career that can help make the world a better place. 

“Understanding the processes and environments of working on a team of researchers would challenge me to develop high-level professional communication and collaboration skills needed for a successful future in nuclear physics. Getting to immerse myself in a scholarly cohort to learn and discuss nuclear physics in the country’s top labs would be the education I’ve always wished for,” said Wolfe.

The program is named in honor of experimental physicist Ray Davis Jr. and theoretical physicist John Bahcall. Davis built a Nobel Prize-winning neutrino experiment at the 4,850-foot level of the former Homestake Mine that is now home to America’s Underground Lab. The experiment was grounded on Bahcall’s solar neutrino theory. More than 150 students have participated in the Davis-Bahcall Scholars Program since 2009.

The Davis-Bahcall Scholar Program is sponsored by the South Dakota Science and Technology Authority, Black Hills State University, First PREMIER Bank, and the South Dakota Space Grant Consortium.

The Institute for Underground Science at SURF seeks to create meaningful pathways and strategic partnerships that strengthen the scientific community and expand opportunities for research and education.

title: John Green is writing his first novel for adults
byline: By Hillel Italie AP National Writer
description: Author John Green poses in his studio in Indianapolis on Jan. 21, 2026. AP Photo, Michael Conroy
_full_text:
John Green is writing his first novel for adults
Author John Green poses in his studio in Indianapolis on Jan. 21, 2026. AP Photo, Michael Conroy 

John Green’s first novel in nearly a decade is also a kind of debut — a work of fiction for adults.

The author of such favorites for young people as “The Fault in Our Stars” and “Looking for Alaska” has written a story about celebrity and the attention economy, “Hollywood, Ending.” Dutton Books, a Penguin Random House imprint, will release the novel on Sept. 22.

According to Dutton, “Hollywood, Ending” centers on two young actors and how their lives change through their roles in the biopic “Andy Warhol Never Gets Old.”

“It’s a book about navigating love and loss all while participating in the strange, complicated exchanges of attention and trauma of the social internet that has become something no longer reserved for movie stars alone,” Green said in a statement released Tuesday.

His most recent novel, “Turtles All the Way Down,” came out in 2017.

During an appearance at Stanford University in 2024, Green said he expected to be moving away from young adult fiction, explaining that he felt more “emotionally and intellectually” distant from the age group.

The 48-year-old author, whose novels have sold millions of copies, knows a few things about fame, and the movie industry. “The Fault in Our Stars” and “Paper Towns” are among his works that have been adapted into films.
